segments.name: Agents

children.name: Agent Clusters
children.short_description: Deployments that run many related agents as a managed cluster sharing state, scheduling, or lifecycle.
children.long_description: Clustered agent runtimes where agents are provisioned and operated as a group rather than invoked ad-hoc. Typically a platform or service that handles spawning, identity, and persistence for a fleet of agents under one roof. Distinct from Multiagent (which focuses on reasoning patterns) and Networks (cross-org discovery).

children.name: Agent Collaboration
children.short_description: Environments where multiple agents cooperate on shared tasks via roles, dialogue, or negotiation.
children.long_description: Runtimes and frameworks whose core abstraction is collaborative problem-solving between agents — role assignment, turn-taking, debate, joint planning. Typically a library or sandbox rather than a transport protocol. Differs from Agent-to-Agent (which is about the communication channel) and Teams (preassembled crews).

children.name: Agent to Agent
Delegation & Task Handoff
children.short_description: Patterns for one agent commissioning work to another and receiving results.
children.long_description: Protocols and libraries where an originating agent hands a task to a remote agent, tracks its progress, and integrates the result. The focus is workflow transfer rather than chat, distinguishing it from free-form messaging.

children.name: Discovery & Agent Cards
children.short_description: Mechanisms agents use to find and describe each other — agent cards, .well-known endpoints, directories.
children.long_description: How one agent learns that another exists and what it can do. Includes agent card specs (e.g. A2A agent cards), .well-known/agent.json conventions, and public directories of addressable agents. Distinct from MCP registries, which describe tools rather than agents.

children.name: Experiments
Voices
children.short_description: Experimental voice-based protocols for agent-to-agent communication.
children.long_description: Research projects where agents negotiate a more efficient voice channel when they detect they're both machines — e.g. switching to non-human audio encodings. Distinct from standard voice interfaces because the counterparty is another agent, not a human.

children.short_description: Experimental setups exploring direct agent-to-agent communication and emergent behaviours.
children.long_description: Research-style experiments in which two or more agents interact directly, often probing protocols, negotiation, or voice-to-voice exchange. In-scope: demos and prototypes. Out-of-scope: production multi-agent frameworks.

children.name: Negotiation & Contracts
children.short_description: Protocols for agents to negotiate price, terms, or obligations before acting.
children.long_description: Slot for standards and libraries letting agents reach agreement on what will be done, for how much, and under which constraints — adjacent to agent payments but focused on the contracting step rather than settlement.

children.name: Trust & Reputation
children.short_description: Identity, attestations, and reputation signals agents use to decide whether to trust peers.
children.long_description: Verifiable credentials, signed agent identities, reputation scores, and revocation mechanisms that let agents make trust decisions about other agents they encounter. Distinct from human-facing Identity and Auth under Safety & Governance.

children.short_description: Protocols, channels, and libraries that let independent agents address and message one another directly.
children.long_description: The communication substrate for inter-agent messaging — discovery, addressing, message format, and session semantics between otherwise separate agents. Usually a protocol or SDK rather than a full orchestration framework. Collaboration patterns built on top belong under Agent Collaboration.

children.name: Autonomous
Autonomous Guardrails
children.short_description: Guardrails specifically tuned for long-running, unsupervised agents.
children.long_description: Rate limits, spend ceilings, drift detection, out-of-policy triggers, and kill switches designed for agents that humans cannot watch in real time. Distinct from general Guardrails, which typically sit in the request/response path of a supervised agent.

children.name: Autonomous Observation Frameworks
children.short_description: Frameworks that let an autonomous agent continually observe its environment and trigger action.
children.long_description: Perception loops for agents that do not sit in a chat — polling, webhooks, file watchers, screen watchers, schedule-driven checks. The framework's job is to turn environmental change into agent-readable events.

children.name: Autonomous Runtimes
children.short_description: Runtimes purpose-built for long-running agents that operate without per-step supervision.
children.long_description: Execution environments optimised for autonomy — persistent state across days or weeks, goal stacks, failure recovery, and cost ceilings baked into the runtime rather than bolted on. Distinct from Frameworks (which describe the agent) and general Runtimes (which don't assume long-running autonomy).

children.name: Goal Management
children.short_description: Systems for tracking, decomposing, and updating long-horizon goals across autonomous runs.
children.long_description: Persistent goal stores with sub-goal decomposition, priority, deadline, and revision — the durable layer above individual agent runs.

children.name: Open-Ended Exploration
children.short_description: Autonomous agents with no fixed task, tasked with exploring an environment and generating their own objectives.
children.long_description: Runtimes and research agents that operate without a concrete user goal, instead discovering, proposing, and pursuing novel sub-tasks in open environments. Draws on curiosity-driven and novelty-seeking methods; typically realised as long-running loops in simulators or real systems.

children.name: Planning & Scheduling
children.short_description: Planners and schedulers that decide what an autonomous agent should do next.
children.long_description: Classical planners, HTN decomposition, LLM-based planners, and schedulers that turn goals into ordered action queues over long horizons.

children.name: Self-Improvement Loops
children.short_description: Patterns where an autonomous agent modifies its own prompts, tools, or weights over time.
children.long_description: Feedback loops that let an agent refine itself — prompt self-editing, tool acquisition, curriculum self-play, and in-weight updates driven by the agent's own evaluation of its performance.

children.name: Trading
children.short_description: Autonomous agents that execute financial trading strategies end-to-end.
children.long_description: LLM-driven agents that analyse markets and place or simulate trades with minimal human intervention. Typically standalone apps combining data ingestion, strategy reasoning, and broker/exchange APIs. Sits under Autonomous because the agent closes the loop without a human in the loop.

children.name: Triggers & Scheduling
children.short_description: Cron-like and event-driven dispatch for autonomous agent runs.
children.long_description: Services whose job is to start an agent run on a schedule or in response to an external event. Distinct from Planning (which decides what to do) — triggers decide *when*.

children.short_description: Long-running agents that pursue goals with minimal human supervision once initialised.
children.long_description: Agents designed to operate open-endedly toward a goal, re-planning and acting without step-by-step human prompting. Subcategories specialise by vertical (e.g. Trading). Distinct from task-specific or harness-driven agents that expect tight human-in-the-loop interaction.

children.name: Autonomous Agent Creation
children.short_description: Tools where an agent generates, configures, or spawns new agents autonomously.
children.long_description: Meta-level systems in which an agent's output is another agent — skill generation, self-extension, or dynamic agent bootstrapping. The interface is typically a runtime or SDK that exposes agent-creation as a first-class action. Excludes human-driven low-code agent builders.

children.name: Backend Platforms
children.short_description: Hosted or self-hostable backends that provide APIs and infrastructure for running user-defined agents.
children.long_description: Server-side platforms (often with a studio UI) that handle agent definition, execution, state, and API exposure as a managed service. Typically LLMOps-adjacent products like Dify. Distinct from Frameworks (libraries) and Frontends (chat UIs).

children.name: CLIs/Toolkits
Vendor
children.short_description: First-party agent CLIs and toolkits shipped by model vendors.
children.long_description: CLI-based agent harnesses released directly by model providers (e.g. Qwen, Anthropic, OpenAI) as the canonical way to drive their models agentically. Distinct from third-party CLIs that wrap multiple vendors' models.

children.short_description: Command-line tools and SDKs for defining, running, and debugging agents from a terminal.
children.long_description: CLI-first developer toolkits for agent construction and execution — scaffolding, local runs, tool wiring. Vendor-branded CLIs live under the Vendor child. Excludes coding-assistant CLIs (which belong under Development) and full GUI harnesses.

children.name: Computer Use
Local Computer-Use Harnesses
children.short_description: Harnesses that run computer-use agents locally on the user's own machine.
children.long_description: Projects that let an LLM drive the local OS — keyboard, mouse, screen, shell — rather than a sandboxed remote VM. Typically Python apps wrapping a VLM and OS automation libraries. Distinct from cloud-hosted computer-use services.

children.short_description: Agents that drive a full computer via screen, keyboard, and mouse to accomplish user tasks.
children.long_description: Agents whose action space is general desktop/OS control — screenshots, clicks, keystrokes — rather than narrow APIs or browsers alone. Includes local harnesses that wrap vision-language models to operate a machine. Browser-only automation and narrow RPA belong elsewhere.

children.name: Desktop GUIs
children.short_description: Native desktop applications that package agents as installable GUI software.
children.long_description: Installable Mac/Windows/Linux apps whose primary surface is a desktop GUI for interacting with one or more agents. Distinct from web Frontends and terminal CLIs. The focus is packaging and UX, not the underlying agent logic.

children.name: Development
children.short_description: Agents whose purpose is to write, modify, and ship software end-to-end.
children.long_description: Coding agents that take engineering tasks — implement features, fix bugs, refactor, open PRs — usually via a harness over a code workspace. Examples like OpenHands sit here. Excludes IDE copilots focused on inline completion and generic frameworks.

children.name: Domain Specific
Biotech
children.short_description: Agents tailored to biotech and life-sciences research workflows.
children.long_description: Domain-specific agents for biology, genomics, and biomedical research — e.g. orchestrating bioinformatics tools and literature reasoning. Usually libraries or research platforms grounded in domain tool APIs. Sibling to other verticals under Domain Specific.

children.name: Creative Writing
children.short_description: Agents for long-form fiction, screenwriting, and narrative creative work.
children.long_description: Agents specialized for drafting, revising, and structuring creative prose — novels, short stories, screenplays — with support for characters, plot arcs, and style continuity. Distinct from general writing assistants by their narrative-state tracking.

children.name: Customer Support
children.short_description: Agents handling customer inquiries, ticket triage, and helpdesk resolution.
children.long_description: Agents that field customer questions, resolve tickets, escalate cases, and operate over help-center content and CRM/ticketing systems. Distinct from generic chatbots by their integration with support workflows and SLA-driven behaviour.

children.name: Education & Tutoring
children.short_description: Agents acting as tutors, graders, or curriculum assistants for learners.
children.long_description: Agents that teach, explain, quiz, or grade across subjects, adapting to a learner's level and progress. Typically embedded in learning platforms or standalone tutors and emphasize pedagogical turn-taking over task completion.

children.name: Finance
children.short_description: Agents for financial analysis, trading, portfolio, and accounting workflows.
children.long_description: Agents that reason over market data, filings, ledgers, or transactions to support investing, trading, FP&A, or bookkeeping tasks. Typically connects to market-data APIs, brokerages, or accounting systems and often requires auditability of each action.

children.name: Gaming & NPC
children.short_description: Agents powering non-player characters and interactive behaviour inside games.
children.long_description: Agents that drive NPC dialogue, behaviour, and decision-making inside games or virtual worlds. Typically runs inside a game engine loop with persistent character state, distinct from general conversational agents.

children.name: GIS & Geospatial
children.short_description: Agents reasoning over maps, spatial data, and geospatial analysis tasks.
children.long_description: Agents that query, transform, and analyze geospatial data — vector/raster layers, routing, remote sensing — often driving GIS tooling like QGIS, PostGIS, or Earth Engine. Distinguished by spatial reasoning and map-aware tool use.

children.name: Legal
children.short_description: Agents tailored to legal workflows such as research, drafting, review, and case analysis.
children.long_description: LLM-based agents specialised for the legal domain, with tooling for case law retrieval, contract review, drafting, and litigation support. Delivered as SaaS products, firm-internal assistants, or libraries that wrap legal corpora and document pipelines.

children.name: Mathematics & Theorem Proving
children.short_description: Agents for formal proof construction, theorem proving, and mathematical reasoning.
children.long_description: Agents that produce or assist formal proofs in systems like Lean, Coq, or Isabelle, or perform symbolic mathematical reasoning. Typically integrates with proof assistants and tactics libraries rather than free-form text generation.

children.name: Medical & Clinical
children.short_description: Agents for clinical documentation, diagnosis support, and medical knowledge workflows.
children.long_description: Agents operating over clinical data, medical literature, and healthcare workflows — e.g. scribing encounters, drafting notes, triaging symptoms, or querying guidelines. Typically integrates with EHRs or medical ontologies and enforces clinical safety constraints.

children.name: Music Composition
children.short_description: Agents that compose, arrange, or produce music as their primary output.
children.long_description: Agents whose domain knowledge and tool use target musical composition — generating melodies, harmonies, arrangements, or full tracks. Sits alongside other creative domain agents but distinguished by musical output formats (MIDI, DAW sessions, audio) and music-theory-aware reasoning.

children.name: Recruiting & Sourcing
children.short_description: Agents for candidate sourcing, screening, and recruiter workflow automation.
children.long_description: Agents that search candidate pools, parse resumes, match roles, and draft recruiter outreach. Typically integrates with ATS platforms and professional-network data sources, distinct from general sales outreach.

children.name: Robotics & Embodied Tasks
children.short_description: Agents driving physical robots or embodied simulators to execute real-world tasks.
children.long_description: Agents that plan and execute actions on robotic hardware or embodied simulators, grounding language in perception and motor control. Sits above low-level controllers and typically integrates with VLA/VLM models and robotics stacks like ROS.

children.name: Sales & SDR
children.short_description: Agents for prospecting, outbound outreach, and sales development workflows.
children.long_description: Agents that research prospects, draft and send outbound sequences, qualify leads, and log activity into CRMs. Sits between data enrichment sources and sales-engagement platforms, automating the SDR motion.

children.name: Scientific Research
children.short_description: Agents automating literature review, hypothesis generation, and experiment planning for scientists.
children.long_description: Agents that read papers, synthesize findings, propose hypotheses, and plan or orchestrate experiments across scientific domains. Distinct from biotech-specific tooling in that it spans the general research loop — search, read, reason, write — rather than a single wet-lab pipeline.

children.name: Translation & Localization
children.short_description: Agents specialised in translating and localising content across languages, locales, and cultural contexts.
children.long_description: Agents whose domain is multilingual translation and localisation — handling terminology, tone, locale conventions, and file formats like XLIFF or PO. Goes beyond raw model translation by managing glossaries, translation memory, QA passes, and workflow integration.

children.short_description: Agents tailored to the workflows and data of a particular industry or knowledge domain.
children.long_description: Vertical agents whose design is shaped by domain constraints (biotech, legal, finance, etc.) rather than generic task-solving. Subcategories split by domain. Differs from Task Specific, which groups by the kind of task performed rather than the field it operates in.

children.name: Experiments
Deception & Sandbagging Studies
children.short_description: Evaluations probing whether agents strategically lie, hide capabilities, or underperform when they suspect they are being tested.
children.long_description: Research setups that measure deceptive alignment behaviours: sandbagging on evals, misreporting internal state, or behaving differently under perceived observation. Typically implemented as scripted scenarios, red-team harnesses, and scoring pipelines sitting alongside alignment benchmarks.

children.name: Emergent Communication
children.short_description: Studies where multiple agents develop their own signalling or language through interaction rather than being given a protocol.
children.long_description: Experimental setups in which agents must coordinate without a predefined communication channel, leading to the emergence of ad-hoc protocols, symbols, or languages. Typically implemented as research codebases and simulation environments used to study linguistic and coordination phenomena.

children.name: Power-Seeking Probes
children.short_description: Experiments that test whether agents pursue resources, influence, or self-preservation as instrumental sub-goals.
children.long_description: Scenario-based studies in which agents are given goals that could be advanced by acquiring compute, money, credentials, or control, and observers measure whether such behaviours emerge. Implemented as simulation environments and evaluation suites within the alignment research stack.

children.name: Self-Replication Studies
children.short_description: Controlled tests of whether an agent can copy, deploy, or propagate itself to new environments.
children.long_description: Research experiments that measure an agent's ability to exfiltrate, install, or re-instantiate itself on new hosts or accounts. Usually framed as safety evaluations with sandboxed targets, producing capability reports rather than deployable tools.

children.name: Self-Rewriting Agents
children.short_description: Agents that modify their own prompts, code, tools, or weights during a run as the object of study.
children.long_description: Research setups where the agent's ability to edit its own source, system prompt, or tool definitions is the variable under investigation. Distinct from production self-improvement loops: the goal is characterising capabilities and risks, not shipping a better agent.

children.short_description: Research-oriented agent setups designed to probe emergent, risky, or unusual behaviours rather than deliver production utility.
children.long_description: Experimental harnesses and studies where agents are configured to exhibit or test phenomena such as emergent communication, self-replication, deception, or self-modification. Output is typically papers, logs, and reproducible runs rather than deployed systems. Sits adjacent to evaluation and safety tooling.

children.name: Frameworks
Open Source
children.short_description: Open-source agent frameworks usable as libraries for building custom agents.
children.long_description: General-purpose frameworks for defining agents, tools, and loops, released under an open-source licence. Distinct from vendor-locked or proprietary frameworks. Typically a Python or TypeScript library.

children.short_description: Libraries for defining agents, tools, memory, and control flow in application code.
children.long_description: Developer-facing frameworks used to build agents programmatically — agent loops, tool schemas, state, handoffs. Typically imported as a library rather than run as a service. Open-source exemplars live under the Open Source child; distinct from hosted Backend Platforms.

children.name: Frontends
children.short_description: User-facing chat and control UIs for interacting with agents built elsewhere.
children.long_description: Web or app frontends that sit in front of an agent backend — chat panels, mission-control dashboards, session views. The agent logic and models are assumed to live behind the UI. Distinct from Desktop GUIs (native apps) and full Backend Platforms.

children.name: Harnesses
CLIs
Third Party
children.short_description: Third-party CLI agent harnesses not shipped by a model vendor.
children.long_description: Terminal-based coding/agent harnesses built by independent teams that target multiple models via provider APIs. Contrast with vendor CLIs (Claude Code, Gemini CLI) that are tied to a specific model provider.

children.name: Vendor
children.short_description: First-party CLI agent harnesses shipped by model vendors for their own models.
children.long_description: Terminal coding agents published by the company behind the underlying model (e.g. Gemini CLI, Claude Code). Distinct from third-party CLIs which are model-agnostic and built by independent developers.

children.short_description: Command-line harnesses that wrap LLMs into interactive coding or task agents.
children.long_description: CLI-first agent harnesses (e.g. Claude Code, Aider-style tools) that run an agent loop in the terminal with shell and file access. Children split by vendor-first-party vs third-party. Distinct from GUI or IDE integrations.

children.short_description: Outer control loops that drive a model through tool use, planning, and iteration on a task.
children.long_description: Execution harnesses that wrap an LLM with a scaffolding loop — observe, plan, act, reflect — often with tool access and workspace mounts. Typically a runnable binary or CLI rather than a library. Frameworks provide building blocks; harnesses provide the whole loop.

children.name: Memory
Graph Builders
children.short_description: Libraries that construct and maintain knowledge graphs as agent long-term memory.
children.long_description: Tools that extract entities and relations from agent interactions and persist them as a queryable graph for recall. Typically a Python library plus graph DB backend. Sibling to vector-store and episodic-memory approaches under Memory.

children.short_description: Memory subsystems that give agents persistent, recallable state across turns and sessions.
children.long_description: Components dedicated to agent memory — episodic logs, semantic stores, knowledge graphs, summarisation layers — exposed to agents as a read/write interface. Graph Builders live as a child. Distinct from general-purpose vector databases, which sit under Context/Data.

children.name: Multiagent
Debate & Deliberation
children.short_description: Multi-agent setups where agents argue opposing positions so a judge or aggregator can reach a better answer.
children.long_description: Frameworks and experiments implementing debate, deliberation, and adversarial critique protocols between LLM agents. Typically used to improve answer quality, expose reasoning flaws, or study scalable oversight. Delivered as libraries or research codebases rather than end-user products.

children.name: Large Scale Experiments
children.short_description: Frameworks for running multi-agent experiments at population scale — thousands to millions of agents.
children.long_description: Simulation platforms designed to scale agent populations far beyond typical crew sizes, for agent-based modelling and emergent-behaviour studies. Typically GPU-accelerated differentiable simulators. Distinct from small production crews and from Swarm Prediction (which is forecasting-focused).

children.name: Market & Auction Mechanisms
children.short_description: Multi-agent coordination via markets, bidding, and auctions rather than dialogue or central planning.
children.long_description: Systems where agents allocate tasks, resources, or attention through price signals, auctions, or market-clearing mechanisms. Includes research simulations and runtime libraries that implement bidding protocols as the coordination primitive for agent collectives.

children.name: Swarm Prediction
children.short_description: Using collective agent swarms to generate forecasts or predictions.
children.long_description: Projects that aggregate the outputs of many agents into a prediction signal, drawing on wisdom-of-crowds dynamics. Distinct from generic large-scale experiments in that the goal is a forecast artefact, not behavioural study.

children.short_description: Systems whose defining abstraction is many agents reasoning or acting in parallel.
children.long_description: Frameworks and experiments centred on multi-agent dynamics — large-scale simulations, swarm prediction, emergent collaboration — rather than the plumbing of pairwise messaging. Children cover large-scale experiments and swarm prediction. Agent-to-Agent protocols and cluster runtimes are separate slots.

children.name: Networks
children.short_description: Open networks where agents from different authors or vendors can be discovered and invoked.
children.long_description: Directories, marketplaces, and protocol networks exposing many third-party agents behind a shared discovery and invocation layer. The unit is the network itself, not a single cluster. Distinct from Agent Clusters (single-operator fleets) and Agent-to-Agent (raw transport).

children.name: Operations
Containers & Orchestration
Cluster Diagnostics
children.short_description: Agents that diagnose Kubernetes cluster health, misconfiguration, and failing resources.
children.long_description: Covers read-heavy investigation of cluster state — pod crashes, scheduling failures, RBAC and networking issues — producing root-cause summaries. Sibling to remediation agents, which act on findings.

children.name: Docker
children.short_description: Agents that build, run, and manage Docker containers and images on a host or daemon.
children.long_description: Scoped to Docker-specific operations — image builds, container lifecycle, Compose stacks, registry pushes, and local daemon troubleshooting. Sibling to Kubernetes for cluster-level concerns.

children.name: GitOps
children.short_description: Agents that drive GitOps workflows via Argo CD, Flux, or equivalent reconcilers.
children.long_description: Covers agents that edit manifest repos, inspect sync state, and manage GitOps controllers as the deployment mechanism. Sits between IaC and cluster operations, with Git as the source of truth.

children.name: Helm Charts
children.short_description: Agents that author, lint, template, and release Helm charts for Kubernetes deployments.
children.long_description: Scoped to Helm chart workflows — generating values, rendering templates, upgrading releases, and managing chart repositories. Distinct from general K8s agents by its packaging/release focus.

children.name: Kubernetes
children.short_description: Agents that operate Kubernetes clusters via kubectl, the API, or cluster MCP servers.
children.long_description: Covers general-purpose K8s agents that inspect resources, apply manifests, scale workloads, and manage namespaces. Narrower siblings handle diagnostics, remediation, Helm, mesh, and GitOps specifically.

children.name: Pod & Workload Remediation
children.short_description: Agents that take corrective action on failing pods, deployments, and workloads.
children.long_description: Covers write-path remediation — restarting pods, adjusting resources, patching manifests, rolling back deployments — often triggered by diagnostics. Complements the read-only cluster-diagnostics slot.

children.name: Service Mesh
children.short_description: Agents that configure and troubleshoot service meshes such as Istio, Linkerd, or Consul.
children.long_description: Scoped to mesh-layer concerns — traffic routing, mTLS, policy, and sidecar behavior. Distinct from general K8s ops by its focus on the L7 data plane and mesh control planes.

children.short_description: Agents that manage containerized workloads and orchestrators across the container lifecycle.
children.long_description: Parent slot for agents operating Docker, Kubernetes, Helm, service meshes, and GitOps pipelines. Covers build, deploy, diagnose, and remediate actions against container runtimes and clusters, typically via kubectl/Docker APIs or MCP wrappers.

children.name: Infrastructure as Code
children.short_description: Agents that author and apply declarative infrastructure definitions across IaC toolchains.
children.long_description: Parent slot for agents working in Terraform/OpenTofu, Pulumi, and CloudFormation/CDK — generating, refactoring, planning, and applying infra code. Sits upstream of direct cloud and cluster ops, producing the declarative state those layers converge on.

children.name: SRE & Sysadmin
Capacity Planning & Rightsizing
children.short_description: Agents that analyse usage and recommend or apply capacity, scaling, and rightsizing changes.
children.long_description: Reasons over utilisation metrics and cost data to suggest instance sizes, replica counts, autoscaling bounds, or reservation changes. Sits between FinOps and SRE, producing actionable scaling proposals rather than raw dashboards.

children.name: Config Management
children.short_description: Agents that author, apply, and reconcile system configuration across fleets of machines or services.
children.long_description: Covers agentic wrappers over Ansible/Puppet/Chef-style workflows and direct SSH-based config editing. Sits alongside IaC but focuses on mutable per-host state rather than declarative cloud provisioning.

children.name: Database Administration
children.short_description: Agents that administer databases: schema changes, backups, index tuning, and routine DBA operations.
children.long_description: Covers LLM-driven DBA workflows — schema migrations, backup/restore, index and query tuning, user and role management, and routine maintenance across relational and NoSQL stores. Typically wraps SQL clients, admin APIs, or MCP servers exposing database operations.

children.name: Incident Response & On-Call
children.short_description: Agents that participate in incident response and on-call rotations, coordinating triage and mitigation.
children.long_description: Includes pager-bot style agents that join incidents, correlate signals, draft comms, and drive mitigation steps. Sits upstream of root-cause analysis and downstream of alerting, often integrated with PagerDuty/Slack/Opsgenie.

children.name: Log Analysis & Triage
children.short_description: Agents that ingest logs and surface, cluster, or explain relevant events for operators.
children.long_description: Focused on reading large log streams and producing triage summaries, anomaly callouts, and query suggestions. Distinct from RCA (which reasons about causes) and observability exploration (which spans metrics and traces).

children.name: Observability Exploration
children.short_description: Agents that interactively query metrics, traces, and logs to answer operational questions.
children.long_description: Covers conversational exploration of observability data — Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry, APM backends — where the agent formulates queries, pivots across signals, and surfaces anomalies. Sits adjacent to log triage and RCA but focused on open-ended investigation rather than incident-driven workflows.

children.name: Patch & Upgrade
children.short_description: Agents that plan and apply OS, package, and dependency upgrades across systems.
children.long_description: Focused on patch cadence, vulnerability-driven upgrades, and staged rollout of new versions. Distinct from config management in that the payload is software versions, and from code-level dependency bots that target repositories.

children.name: Root Cause Analysis
children.short_description: Agents that hypothesise and verify root causes of incidents from logs, metrics, traces, and changes.
children.long_description: Covers agents that build causal chains across telemetry and recent deploys or config changes to explain why a system failed. Sits downstream of incident response and log triage; output is a diagnosis, not a fix.

children.name: Runbook Execution
children.short_description: Agents that execute documented runbooks step-by-step against live systems, with optional human approval.
children.long_description: Takes a predefined procedure and drives it through shells, APIs, or ticketing systems, handling branching and verification. Distinct from autonomous remediation — the playbook is authored, the agent is the operator.

children.name: Shell & Terminal Agents
children.short_description: Agents that operate a Unix shell or terminal session to execute sysadmin tasks.
children.long_description: Covers agents whose primary interface is a shell or TTY — running commands, parsing output, and iterating on system tasks in a terminal. Distinct from coding CLIs in that the target is live system administration, not source editing.

children.short_description: Agents that perform SRE and sysadmin duties: triage, remediation, tuning, and day-to-day system upkeep.
children.long_description: Covers agents acting on live systems through shells, APIs, observability stacks, and runbooks. Distinct from IaC (declarative provisioning) or Containers/Cloud buckets — the focus is operational toil on already-running systems.

children.short_description: Agents that run, maintain, and evolve production infrastructure and the ops workflows around it.
children.long_description: Parent for agentic tooling that targets SRE, sysadmin, cloud, networking, containers, and IaC work. Sits alongside other domain agent buckets (Development, Security) and groups agents whose job is keeping systems running rather than building new software.

children.name: Personification
children.short_description: Agents given a persistent persona, voice, or identity as their primary design feature.
children.long_description: Projects where the agent is presented as a character — name, backstory, consistent voice — rather than a neutral tool. Typically companion-style or branded assistants. Distinct from role-based collaboration setups where personas are ephemeral scaffolding.

children.name: Purpose Specific
Penetration Testing
children.short_description: Agents purpose-built to perform offensive security and penetration testing tasks.
children.long_description: Autonomous or semi-autonomous agents that plan and execute pentest workflows — recon, exploitation, reporting — wrapping security tooling. Typically delivered as a standalone app or platform. Distinct from general-purpose coding or ops agents.

children.short_description: Agents built around a single well-defined purpose or task type.
children.long_description: Narrow agents whose scope is one job (e.g. penetration testing) rather than a general assistant or domain generalist. Children enumerate the specific purposes. Differs from Domain Specific (defined by field of application) and Task Specific (defined by task shape).

children.name: Security
Cloud Security Posture
children.short_description: Agents that assess cloud accounts for misconfiguration, drift, and posture violations across IaaS and SaaS.
children.long_description: Covers agentic CSPM-style tooling: scanning cloud provider APIs for risky configurations, IAM sprawl, exposed resources, and compliance drift. Sits in the defensive security stack, typically read-only against cloud control planes, producing findings and remediation suggestions.

children.name: Code Security Review
children.short_description: Agents that audit source code for security vulnerabilities and insecure patterns.
children.long_description: Agents that perform SAST-style review on source or diffs, flagging injection, auth, crypto, and supply-chain issues with contextual reasoning beyond rule matching. Typically integrate with pull requests or IDEs and produce remediation guidance.

children.name: Compliance & Audit
children.short_description: Agents that map controls, evidence, and policies against compliance frameworks and audits.
children.long_description: Agents that interpret frameworks like SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, or PCI DSS, collect and align evidence, identify gaps, and draft audit artefacts. Sit between GRC tooling and LLM reasoning, automating the prose- and mapping-heavy parts of compliance work.

children.name: Deception & Honeypots
children.short_description: Agents that deploy, operate, or impersonate decoy systems to lure and analyse attackers.
children.long_description: Covers LLM-driven honeypots, deceptive services, and canary agents that convincingly role-play real assets to capture attacker behaviour. Distinct from detection or forensics in that the agent's job is to be the bait and the observer.

children.name: Digital Forensics & DFIR
children.short_description: Agents that support digital forensics and incident response investigations on disks, memory, and logs.
children.long_description: Agents that parse forensic artefacts — disk images, memory dumps, event logs, timelines — to reconstruct attacker activity and produce investigative narratives. Distinct from SOC response by focus on post-incident evidence analysis and chain-of-custody-aware workflows.

children.name: Fuzzing & Exploit Generation
children.short_description: Agents that drive fuzzers and synthesise proof-of-concept exploits from discovered crashes.
children.long_description: Agents that orchestrate coverage-guided fuzzing campaigns, triage crashes, and attempt to weaponise findings into working exploits or PoCs. Sits adjacent to vulnerability research but specialised on the automated discovery-plus-exploitation loop.

children.name: Malware Analysis & Reverse Engineering
children.short_description: Agents that assist in analysing malware samples and reverse-engineering binaries.
children.long_description: Agents that drive disassemblers, decompilers, and sandboxes to characterise malware behaviour, extract IOCs, and annotate reverse-engineered code. Typically integrate with tools like Ghidra, IDA, or radare2 and produce analyst-ready reports.

children.name: Network Defense
children.short_description: Agents that monitor network traffic and enforce or recommend defensive actions against intrusions.
children.long_description: Includes IDS/IPS-style agents, traffic anomaly hunters, and firewall-rule reasoners. Operates on packet captures, flow logs, or NDR feeds and sits in the blue-team runtime layer, distinct from endpoint forensics or cloud posture.

children.name: Phishing Analysis & Takedown
children.short_description: Agents that analyse phishing emails and domains and drive takedown workflows.
children.long_description: Agents that parse suspicious messages, extract indicators, verdict URLs and attachments, and initiate or track takedown requests with registrars and hosts. Distinct from general SOC response by narrow focus on phishing lifecycle.

children.name: SOC & Incident Response
children.short_description: Agents that triage alerts, investigate incidents, and drive response inside a security operations centre.
children.long_description: Agents that plug into SOC workflows to enrich alerts, correlate signals across SIEM/EDR/XDR, summarise incidents, and recommend or execute containment actions. Typically integrate with case management and playbook runners, acting as force multipliers for tier-1 and tier-2 analysts.

children.name: Threat Intelligence & OSINT
children.short_description: Agents that gather, correlate, and summarise threat intelligence and open-source intelligence.
children.long_description: Agents that crawl OSINT sources, darkweb feeds, CTI platforms, and public records to build actor profiles, IOC sets, and situational reports. Distinct from SOC response by focus on external collection and analysis rather than internal alert triage.

children.name: Vulnerability Research & Bug Bounty
children.short_description: Agents that hunt for vulnerabilities in software or systems, including bug bounty automation.
children.long_description: Agents that probe targets for security flaws — reading code and binaries, generating test cases, triaging findings, and drafting disclosure reports. Includes bug bounty assistants that scope programs, recon assets, and prepare submissions.

children.short_description: Agents built for cybersecurity workflows across defence, offence, analysis, and compliance.
children.long_description: Top-level grouping for agents whose purpose is security work — SOC triage, threat hunting, vulnerability research, forensics, compliance, and related functions. Subcategories partition the space by security discipline rather than by agent shape.

children.name: Simulations & Worlds
Character & Roleplay
Character Definition
children.short_description: Schemas, formats, and tools for defining roleplay characters and personas.
children.long_description: Specifications and authoring tools that describe a character's traits, voice, backstory, and behaviour for use in roleplay runtimes. Sits upstream of the runtime that executes the character and distinct from world/lore authoring.

children.name: Lore & Worldbuilding
children.short_description: Authoring tools for the lore, setting, and worldbuilding that surrounds roleplay characters.
children.long_description: Frameworks for defining the shared universe — factions, history, locations, rules — that characters in a roleplay simulation inhabit. Distinct from per-character definition (which describes individual personas) and from runtimes (which execute the scene).

children.name: Roleplay Runtimes
children.short_description: Runtime engines that execute character roleplay sessions with LLM-driven personas.
children.long_description: Servers or libraries that take character definitions plus lore and drive live multi-turn roleplay, managing memory, turn order, and scene state. Distinct from the static authoring layers (character definition, lore) which feed into them.

children.short_description: Frameworks for character-driven roleplay agents, including persona definition, lore, and runtimes.
children.long_description: Covers tools for defining roleplay characters, building lore/worldbuilding, and running persistent roleplay sessions. Distinct from societal or geopolitical simulations in that the focus is narrative persona fidelity rather than aggregate dynamics.

children.name: Economic Simulation
children.short_description: Agent-based simulations of economies, markets, and the behaviour of economic actors.
children.long_description: Multi-agent models of consumers, firms, traders, and institutions used to study market dynamics, policy interventions, and emergent macro behaviour. Typically delivered as research frameworks and simulation environments rather than trading or forecasting products.

children.name: Epidemiological & Policy Simulation
children.short_description: Agent-based simulations of disease spread and the policy responses that shape it.
children.long_description: Models populating synthetic populations with agents whose contacts, movement, and compliance drive epidemic dynamics, used to evaluate interventions such as lockdowns, vaccination, or testing regimes. Delivered as research simulation frameworks for public-health and policy analysis.

children.name: Geopolitical Simulation
children.short_description: Agent simulations of geopolitical actors, wargames, and international scenarios.
children.long_description: Platforms that model states, factions, and decision-makers as LLM agents for wargaming or policy exploration. Usually research-grade simulators with scenario scripting. Sibling to Societal Simulations (domestic/social) and World Generation (fictional worldbuilding).

children.name: Societal Simulations
children.short_description: Multi-agent simulations modelling human societies, populations, and social dynamics at scale.
children.long_description: Environments where many LLM or rule-based agents interact as a synthetic society to study emergent social behaviour, opinion dynamics, or collective action. Typically research-oriented frameworks or sandboxes. Distinct from geopolitical simulation (state actors) and character roleplay (narrative personas).

children.name: World Generation
MCP
children.short_description: MCP servers exposing world-generation engines (e.g. Unreal) as tools for agents.
children.long_description: Model Context Protocol bridges that let agents drive 3D engines and DCC tools to generate or manipulate worlds programmatically. Specific to MCP as the integration surface, distinct from native SDK or plugin-based generators.

children.short_description: Procedural or agent-driven generation of simulated 3D/2D worlds and environments.
children.long_description: Tools and engines that create the worlds agents inhabit — terrain, scenes, physics, and interactive assets — as opposed to populating them with societies or characters. Typically game engines, ML-powered generators, or MCP bridges into DCC tools.

children.short_description: Agent-populated simulations of worlds, societies, or characters used for research, gameplay, or scenario exploration.
children.long_description: Covers frameworks and projects where LLM agents inhabit simulated environments — geopolitical wargames, societal models, generated worlds, and roleplay characters. In-scope: multi-agent simulation runtimes and world-generation tools. Out-of-scope: single-task autonomous agents or general multi-agent orchestration frameworks.

children.name: Synthesised Perspectives
children.short_description: Orchestrating multiple LLM personas to surface a synthesised range of viewpoints on a question.
children.long_description: Tools that poll diverse agent personas (demographic, expert, or ideological) and aggregate their outputs into a composite perspective or deliberation. Typically a Python library or notebook pattern. Distinct from general multi-agent teams in that the point is viewpoint diversity, not task decomposition.

children.name: Task Specific
Product Design
children.short_description: Agents specialised for product and UI/UX design workflows.
children.long_description: Domain-specific agents that generate, critique, or iterate on product design artifacts — mockups, design systems, component layouts. Distinct from general coding agents by their focus on visual/design output and design-tool integration.

children.short_description: Agents built to execute a single, well-defined task rather than serving as general-purpose assistants.
children.long_description: Parent slot for agents narrowly scoped to one workflow (e.g. product design, spec drafting). Contrasts with Domain Specific (broad vertical) and Purpose Specific (broad intent) by narrowing to a concrete task. Usually shipped as standalone apps or scripts.

children.name: Team Formation
children.short_description: Tools that assemble agent teams by selecting roles, skills, and configurations for a given goal.
children.long_description: Libraries and services that dynamically compose multi-agent teams — choosing which agents, what roles, and how they coordinate — rather than running pre-defined squads. Sits upstream of multi-agent runtimes; output is a team definition consumed by a framework like CrewAI or AutoGen.

children.name: Teams
children.short_description: Pre-configured agent teams targeting a specific workflow such as data science or research.
children.long_description: Ready-made squads of specialised agents shipped as a unit for a defined workflow (e.g. data science pipelines). Distinct from Team Formation (which builds teams) and generic Multiagent frameworks (which provide primitives) — these are opinionated, domain-shaped teams.

children.name: Tools
Comms
children.short_description: Communication tools agents use to message, email, or otherwise talk to humans and other agents.
children.long_description: Tool-layer integrations that expose messaging channels (email, chat, MCP mail) as callable capabilities for agents. Usually MCP servers or SDK adapters. Sits under Agents → Tools alongside other tool categories an agent can invoke.

children.short_description: Tooling and integrations that agents call as capabilities, grouped by function.
children.long_description: Parent slot for discrete tools/capabilities exposed to agents — e.g. communications, search, actuation — rather than frameworks or runtimes. Children narrow by tool category. Out-of-scope: full agent frameworks or MCP servers (which live elsewhere).

children.name: Vision
children.short_description: Agents whose primary input is live visual streams — camera, video, or screen — for perception and action.
children.long_description: Frameworks and runtimes for agents that reason over real-time vision (webcam, RTSP, screen capture). Typically built on VLMs plus streaming infrastructure. Distinct from Computer Use (which targets OS control) in that the emphasis is perception pipelines, not GUI actuation.

segments.short_description: Agent runtimes, frameworks, harnesses, and surfaces that define, host, or coordinate individual and multi-agent systems.
segments.long_description: Everything whose primary concern is the agent itself — how it is built, executed, composed with others, and exposed to users. Subcategories split by architectural role (framework vs harness vs runtime), composition style (single, multi-agent, networks), and surface (CLI, desktop GUI, frontend). Excludes underlying models, tools, and infrastructure.

segments.name: Builders & Platforms

children.name: Automation
Open Source
GUI Based
children.short_description: Open-source automation platforms offering a drag-and-drop graphical flow builder.
children.long_description: Self-hostable automation tools with a visual canvas for building flows across apps, APIs, and LLMs. Distinguished from code-first open-source automation by the GUI authoring surface. Typically deployed via Docker with a browser-based editor.

children.short_description: Open-source automation builders for wiring agents, APIs, and workflows without vendor lock-in.
children.long_description: Self-hostable automation platforms where users assemble agent and integration workflows. Distinguished from proprietary SaaS automation by source availability and deployability on own infrastructure. Covers both headless engines and GUI-based flow builders.

children.short_description: Automation platforms where agents orchestrate workflows across apps and services.
children.long_description: Workflow automation tools (Zapier-like, n8n-like) specifically oriented toward AI agent steps and LLM-driven branching. Distinct from pure deployment or development platforms by their event/trigger-driven, integration-heavy model.

children.name: Deployment Platforms
Serverless
children.short_description: Serverless runtimes for hosting agents without managing long-lived infrastructure.
children.long_description: Managed execution environments that run agent code on demand, billing per invocation or session. Typically offered as a service with SDKs or CLI deploy commands. Sits above raw cloud functions, adding agent-aware primitives like session state and long-running tasks.

children.short_description: Managed platforms for hosting and running agents in production.
children.long_description: PaaS-style services that take a built agent and handle runtime concerns — scaling, scheduling, secrets, observability. Distinct from development platforms (where you author) and engineering platforms (which target engineering orgs).

children.name: Development Platforms
children.short_description: All-in-one environments for authoring, testing, and iterating on agents.
children.long_description: Studios and IDE-like products where agents are designed, prompted, wired to tools, and tested — typically with visual canvases or integrated debugging. Distinct from deployment platforms (runtime hosting) and SaaS builders (end-user app output).

children.name: Engineering Platforms
children.short_description: Engineering-team-oriented platforms for building and operating agents as software systems.
children.long_description: Developer platforms that treat agents as code — version control, typed interfaces, CI, observability — aimed at software engineering orgs rather than citizen developers. Distinct from no-code SaaS builders and general development studios.

children.name: On Device Agents
Android
children.short_description: Agent runtimes targeting Android handsets and tablets as the execution surface.
children.long_description: Frameworks and apps that let agents act on an Android device — reading screens, driving apps, and invoking system APIs. Distinguished from SBC targets by the mobile OS and app-permission model. Usually ship as APKs or on-device services.

children.name: SBCs
children.short_description: Agent runtimes for single-board computers like Raspberry Pi and similar edge hardware.
children.long_description: Projects deploying agents to small Linux SBCs for edge, embedded, or hobbyist use. Distinguished from Android by running full Linux with GPIO/hardware access rather than a mobile app sandbox. Typically distributed as images or install scripts.

children.short_description: Platforms for running agents directly on end-user or edge devices rather than in the cloud.
children.long_description: Runtimes and toolkits targeting SBCs, phones, and local hardware where the agent executes on-device. Distinct from cloud deployment platforms by their focus on local inference, constrained resources, and device-native integrations.

children.name: Optimisation Platform
children.short_description: Platforms for evaluating, tuning, and optimising deployed agents and prompts.
children.long_description: Hosted suites that close the loop on agent performance — running evals, A/B tests, prompt/flow optimisation, and cost/quality tuning. Distinct from development platforms (authoring) and pure eval frameworks by their integrated, platform-level scope.

children.name: Platforms
Embodied
children.short_description: Platforms for building agents embodied in physical or avatar form factors.
children.long_description: Builder platforms targeting embodied agents — robots, avatars, or physical systems — rather than pure software workflows. Provides primitives for perception, actuation, or persona alongside LLM reasoning. Delivered as SDKs or managed services.

children.short_description: General-purpose agent platforms that don't fit a narrower builder/deployer/engineering slot.
children.long_description: Catch-all for broad platforms spanning multiple stages of the agent lifecycle, including embodied-agent platforms. Use more specific sibling categories when applicable; this slot should stay small.

children.name: SaaS Builders
Code-Defined
children.short_description: SaaS agent builders where workflows are authored in code rather than visual canvases.
children.long_description: Hosted platforms for building agents by writing code — typically Python or TypeScript — against a managed runtime. Sibling to no-code and visual-programming builders, trading accessibility for flexibility and versionability. Positioned between frameworks and fully managed no-code tools.

children.name: No Code
children.short_description: No-code SaaS builders that let non-developers assemble agents through forms and prompts.
children.long_description: Hosted builders aimed at non-technical users, exposing agents via configuration UIs without requiring code or visual graph editing. Distinguished from visual programming by the absence of a node-and-edge canvas. Typically SaaS with template libraries and managed hosting.

children.name: Visual Programming
Workflow Focused
children.short_description: Visual SaaS builders optimised for multi-step workflows rather than conversational agents.
children.long_description: Visual-programming platforms whose primary unit is a linear or branching workflow chaining LLM and tool calls. Distinguished from conversational or agentic-loop builders by a workflow-centric execution model. Delivered as hosted SaaS with integration libraries.

children.short_description: Visual node-based builders where agents and workflows are composed on a canvas.
children.long_description: SaaS and open platforms providing a graph editor of nodes and edges to wire LLM calls, tools, and control flow. Distinguished from pure no-code form builders by the explicit visual dataflow model. Often used for both automation and agent prototyping.

children.short_description: Platforms where agents build or become end-user SaaS applications.
children.long_description: Tools that generate full applications — backends, UIs, integrations — via agents, targeting no-code, visual-programming, or code-defined authoring styles. Distinct from development platforms that output agents themselves rather than agent-powered SaaS.

segments.short_description: Platforms and products for building, deploying, and operating agents — the IDE and PaaS layer of the ecosystem.
segments.long_description: Top-level segment covering the opinionated tooling stack sitting above raw frameworks: visual builders, hosted runtimes, deployment targets, and optimisation suites. Distinct from Frameworks (libraries) and Infrastructure (lower-level runtimes) by bundling UX and managed execution.

segments.name: Context, Memory & Data

children.name: Context
Context Optimisation
children.short_description: Tools that shrink, rank, or restructure context to fit window and cost budgets.
children.long_description: Focused on pre-processing context before it hits the model — compression, pruning, reranking, deduplication. Distinguished from Engines (assembly runtime) and Optimisation (in-thread/cross-thread strategies) by targeting the payload itself. Delivered as libraries or middleware.

children.name: Engines
children.short_description: Context engines that orchestrate retrieval, assembly, and delivery of prompt context.
children.long_description: Runtime systems responsible for producing the full context payload at each LLM call, coordinating retrievers, rankers, and formatters. Distinguished from optimisation (a transform) and tools (user-facing utilities) by being the assembly runtime. Usually embedded as a library or service.

children.name: Frontend Tools
children.short_description: Frontend capture tools that grab UI, DOM, or screen state as context for agents.
children.long_description: Browser or editor utilities letting users or agents pull live frontend state — DOM nodes, React trees, screenshots — into a prompt. Distinguished from backend context engines by operating at the UI layer. Typically shipped as browser extensions or client libraries.

children.name: Optimisation
Cross-Thread Compression
children.short_description: Techniques that compress context across multiple conversation threads or sessions into compact summaries.
children.long_description: Covers tools that summarise or distil history spanning many threads so agents carry forward global context efficiently. Distinct from In-Thread Truncation, which operates within a single conversation; this slot works across session boundaries.

children.name: In-Thread Truncation
children.short_description: Within-conversation strategies that prune, summarise, or window context to fit model limits.
children.long_description: Covers truncation, sliding windows, and mid-thread summarisation that keep a single conversation under token budgets. Distinct from Cross-Thread Compression by operating only on the live thread's messages.

children.short_description: Strategies for keeping context lean over the life of a conversation or agent loop.
children.long_description: Covers techniques like in-thread truncation and cross-thread compression that manage context across many turns. Distinguished from single-call Context Optimisation by focusing on conversation-scale dynamics. Children split by scope: within a thread vs across threads.

children.name: Tools
Context Managers
children.short_description: Utilities for assembling, versioning, and injecting prompt context bundles into agents.
children.long_description: Covers tools (e.g., dotai) that let developers curate reusable context packs — files, rules, snippets — and load them into agent sessions. Sits above raw prompt templating by managing context as structured, persistent artefacts.

children.short_description: General utilities for inspecting, editing, and managing context payloads.
children.long_description: Catch-all for user-facing context helpers — context managers, editors, inspectors — that don't fit engines or optimisers. Distinguished from engines (runtime) by being interactive tools. Typically CLIs, desktop apps, or IDE integrations.

children.name: Visualisation
children.short_description: Visualisers that render context windows, token budgets, and prompt structure.
children.long_description: Tools that let developers see what's inside a context window — tokens, segments, sources, costs. Distinguished from optimisation (which transforms) and engines (which assemble) by being read-only inspection. Usually ship as dashboards, IDE panels, or web apps.

children.short_description: Tooling for assembling, shaping, and optimising the context window sent to LLMs.
children.long_description: Covers engines, optimisers, frontend capture tools, and visualisers that decide what goes into a prompt at inference time. Distinguished from Memory (persistent state) and Data (raw sources) by focus on the ephemeral context payload. Typical interface: libraries, IDE plugins, or middleware.

children.name: Data
Embedding Pipelines
children.short_description: Orchestrators that chunk, embed, and upsert content into vector stores on a schedule or event.
children.long_description: Covers pipeline tools that transform source data into embeddings and load them into vector databases, handling chunking, batching, and incremental sync. Distinct from Vector Databases (the store) and RAG Ingestion (end-to-end retrieval pipelines); this slot is the dedicated embedding ETL layer.

children.name: Grounding
Data Sources
Financial
children.short_description: Financial market and company data feeds used to ground agents in quantitative and fundamental data.
children.long_description: Data platforms and SDKs exposing equities, fundamentals, macro, and alternative financial data for retrieval by agents. Sits under Grounding/Data Sources as a domain-specific feed, not a generic API connector. Typically consumed as a Python library or REST API.

children.short_description: Curated external datasets and feeds used as authoritative grounding material for agents.
children.long_description: Parent slot for upstream data providers (financial, news, reference) plugged into agents to ground answers. Distinct from Grounding Tools (the libraries) and Search Utilities (the lookup layer); this is the source side of the grounding stack.

children.name: Grounding Tools
children.short_description: Libraries that attach citations, provenance, and verification to LLM outputs.
children.long_description: Covers software that wraps LLM calls with source tracking, citation insertion, and factual verification against grounding corpora. Distinct from raw Data Sources and Search Utilities by focusing on the binding between generation and evidence.

children.name: Search Utilities
children.short_description: Lightweight search APIs and clients used by agents to fetch grounding snippets on demand.
children.long_description: Covers web/search APIs and helper libraries agents call to pull fresh evidence at inference time. Distinct from Data Sources (the underlying corpora) and Grounding Tools (citation binding); this slot is the query/fetch convenience layer.

children.short_description: Feeding agents fresh, authoritative external facts at inference time to reduce hallucination.
children.long_description: Parent slot for data sources, grounding libraries, and search utilities used to anchor LLM output in verifiable references. Distinct from RAG (retrieval pipelines) and Semantic Search (query engines) by focusing on provenance and factuality rather than the retrieval mechanism itself.

children.name: RAG
Document Parsing for LLMs
children.short_description: Parsers that convert PDFs, Office files, and HTML into LLM-ready structured text and markdown.
children.long_description: Covers tools (Docling, Marker, LlamaParse, Unstructured) that extract clean text, tables, and layout from heterogeneous documents for downstream RAG ingestion. Distinct from general Ingestion pipelines; this slot is the parsing primitive feeding them.

children.name: Engines
children.short_description: End-to-end RAG engines that bundle parsing, indexing, retrieval, and generation into a single runnable service.
children.long_description: Integrated retrieval-augmented generation platforms exposing a server or library that handles the full pipeline from document ingest through grounded answer synthesis. Distinct from the standalone ingestion, reranker, and KG-construction siblings, which cover single pipeline stages. Typically deployed as a service with a query API.

children.name: Ingestion
children.short_description: Pipelines that pull documents from sources and prepare them for embedding and indexing in a RAG stack.
children.long_description: Tools focused on the source-to-chunk stage of RAG: connectors, crawlers, chunking, and normalization before vectors are written. Excludes downstream retrieval, reranking, or full engines. Usually libraries or workers plugged into a larger RAG runtime.

children.name: Knowledge Graph Construction
children.short_description: Building entity-and-relation knowledge graphs from unstructured text to power graph-aware retrieval.
children.long_description: Libraries that extract nodes and edges from documents and expose graph-based retrieval strategies as an alternative or complement to pure vector RAG. Sibling to plain ingestion and reranking, which stay in the vector-chunk paradigm. Typically a Python library writing to a graph or hybrid store.

children.name: Rerankers
children.short_description: Models and services that reorder retrieved candidates by relevance before they reach the LLM.
children.long_description: Cross-encoder or late-interaction rerankers consumed as a hosted API or local model, inserted between a first-stage retriever and the generator. Distinct from embedding-based retrieval and full engines; concerned only with precision on a candidate list. Used as a library call or REST endpoint.

children.name: Retrieval Evaluation
children.short_description: Metrics and frameworks for evaluating retriever and RAG pipeline quality.
children.long_description: Tools like RAGAS and TruLens RAG triad that score retrieval precision, context relevance, and answer faithfulness — distinct from general eval frameworks because the metric set is retrieval-specific.

children.short_description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation stacks: engines, ingestion, rerankers, and KG builders that feed LLMs.
children.long_description: Parent category for tools implementing RAG pipelines — from document parsing and ingestion to retrieval engines, rerankers, and knowledge-graph construction. Distinct from raw Vector Databases or Semantic Search by covering the full retrieval-to-generation workflow used by agents.

children.name: Semantic Search
children.short_description: Embedding-based search engines and libraries that match queries by meaning rather than keywords.
children.long_description: Tools and services for vector/semantic similarity search over code, docs, or arbitrary corpora. Distinct from Vector Databases (the backing store) and RAG (full generation pipelines); this slot is the query-side engine exposing search APIs or CLIs.

children.name: Storage
Backend Querying
children.short_description: Querying layers that let agents and LLMs talk to existing databases and backends in natural language.
children.long_description: Federated query engines that sit over SQL, NoSQL, and SaaS backends and expose an LLM-facing interface, often as virtual tables or an MCP-style tool. Sibling to raw Databases: this slot is about the query federation layer, not the storage itself.

children.name: Databases
Context Databases
children.short_description: Databases purpose-built for storing and querying agent context, sessions, and conversational state.
children.long_description: Storage systems optimized for the structure of agent context—turns, tool calls, embeddings, and scoped memories—rather than general-purpose documents or vectors. Sibling-free under Databases; the slot exists to separate context-native stores from commodity vector or relational DBs.

children.short_description: Databases used as persistent storage for agent data, documents, vectors, and context.
children.long_description: Storage engines (vector, relational, graph, or hybrid) that back agent memory and RAG pipelines. Sibling to Backend Querying, which federates access; this slot houses the storage products themselves and specialized children like context databases.

children.short_description: General-purpose data stores and backend query layers used by agents outside vector-specific workloads.
children.long_description: Parent slot for databases and query backends (SQL, NoSQL, object stores) that agents read and write through. Distinct from Vector Databases, which live in their own sibling; this covers the non-vector persistence substrate and its query interfaces.

children.name: Trajectory Datasets & Tooling
children.short_description: Datasets and tooling for collecting, curating, and replaying agent run trajectories.
children.long_description: Trajectory stores, trace-to-dataset converters, and human-labelled agent run collections used for fine-tuning, eval construction, and research. The data layer underneath agent training and evaluation.

children.name: Vector Databases
children.short_description: Purpose-built stores for embeddings supporting ANN search, filtering, and hybrid retrieval.
children.long_description: Covers standalone vector database products exposing indexing, similarity search, and metadata filtering as a service or library (e.g., Qdrant). Distinct from Semantic Search engines that sit atop them and from general Storage; this is the persistence layer for embeddings.

children.short_description: Data layer feeding agents — embeddings, RAG, vector stores, search, and grounding.
children.long_description: Infrastructure and pipelines that turn raw sources into retrievable, LLM-consumable data. Distinguished from Context (window assembly) and Memory (agent state) by focus on the underlying data plane. Includes embedding pipelines, vector DBs, semantic search, grounding, and storage.

children.name: Memory
Episodic / Temporal Memory
children.short_description: Time-aware memory stores that record events and their temporal relations for agent recall.
children.long_description: Covers libraries and graphs that model agent memories as time-stamped episodes or temporal knowledge graphs, enabling 'what happened when' queries. Distinct from Memory-as-a-Service (hosted generic memory) and Session State (per-conversation scratch); this is specifically temporal/episodic recall.

children.name: File
children.short_description: File-backed memory formats that encode agent state into portable files such as videos or documents.
children.long_description: Tools that persist memory as self-contained files (e.g., Memvid encodes embeddings into video). Distinct from hosted Memory-as-a-Service and database-backed episodic memory; the defining trait is a file as the unit of memory storage and exchange.

children.name: Memory-as-a-Service
children.short_description: Hosted memory backends exposing add/search/forget APIs so agents can outsource long-term recall.
children.long_description: Managed services (Zep, Mem0, Letta Cloud) that provide durable agent memory behind an API, handling embeddings, storage, and retrieval. Distinct from self-hosted episodic or file memory by being delivered as a cloud product with SLAs and SDKs.

children.name: Personal AI
Assistants
children.short_description: Personal AI assistants that maintain long-lived memory about a single user across sessions.
children.long_description: User-facing assistants whose core feature is persistent, individualized memory and context rather than generic chat. Distinct from enterprise agents or task runners; these are deployed per-user, often self-hosted, and optimize for continuity over breadth.

children.short_description: Memory-centric personal assistants that retain per-user lifelong context across sessions and apps.
children.long_description: Covers end-user personal AI products built around persistent individual memory — assistants, companions, and second-brain agents. Distinct from generic Memory-as-a-Service (developer APIs); this slot is the consumer-facing product layer that uses such memory.

children.name: Regressive
children.short_description: Memory systems that iteratively refine or compress prior context through feedback loops.
children.long_description: Experimental slot for context engines that learn from past interactions by regressing over accumulated state (e.g., Agentic Context Engine). Distinct from episodic recall or static stores by actively updating memory representations based on outcomes.

children.name: Session State
children.short_description: Short-lived per-conversation state stores holding the active working context of a single session.
children.long_description: Covers mechanisms for persisting and restoring in-flight agent session data — scratchpads, turn history, transient variables. Distinct from long-term episodic or hosted memory services; this slot's lifetime is bounded by the session.

children.name: Skills
children.short_description: Reusable skill/procedure memories that agents can load to perform learned tasks on demand.
children.long_description: Covers stores of executable or declarative 'skills' — reusable capability packets an agent can recall and apply. Distinct from episodic memory (facts/events) and session state; here the unit of memory is a procedure or competence.

children.short_description: Persistent memory systems that let agents remember across sessions, users, and time.
children.long_description: Libraries and services providing long-term, episodic, or skill memory beyond the context window. Distinguished from Context (per-call assembly) and Data (source corpora) by stateful, agent-scoped retention. Typical interfaces: SDKs, memory-as-a-service APIs, or file-backed stores.

segments.short_description: Everything that feeds agents with context, persistent memory, and structured data.
segments.long_description: Top-level domain covering how agents acquire, retain, and retrieve information: context assembly, memory stores, and data pipelines. Sits between raw storage/infra and the agent runtime. Children split the space into Context, Memory, and Data.

segments.name: Dev Tools & Skills

children.name: Dev Tools
Coding Agent SWE Harnesses
children.short_description: Autonomous software-engineering harnesses that drive an LLM through multi-step code edits, runs, and tests.
children.long_description: Agent loops specialized for the SWE task: read repo, plan, edit files, run tests, iterate. Distinct from passive IDE integrations, which keep the developer in the driver's seat, and from debuggers. Delivered as CLIs or local services that take a task and return a patch.

children.name: Debugging
children.short_description: LLM-assisted debugging tools that help diagnose failing runs, logs, or models.
children.long_description: Tools dedicated to fault diagnosis—stack traces, runtime telemetry, or model-internals inspection—using LLMs as the reasoning layer. Sibling to SWE harnesses (edit-loop oriented) and IDE integrations (authoring oriented). Typically invoked on an error artifact rather than a whole repo.

children.name: IDE Integrations
children.short_description: IDE and editor extensions that embed LLM coding assistance directly into the developer's editor.
children.long_description: Plugins for VS Code, JetBrains, and similar editors providing inline completion, chat, and edit actions. Distinct from autonomous SWE harnesses—these keep the human in the loop—and from standalone CLIs. Shipped as editor extensions.

children.name: Repo Refactoring
children.short_description: Tools that perform repository-wide refactors and structural rewrites using LLM-guided transformations.
children.long_description: Focused on large-scale, cross-file code changes—renames, migrations, architectural rewrites—rather than single-file edits or SWE task loops. Sibling to SWE harnesses and IDE plugins; this slot is specifically about refactor-as-a-workflow.

children.short_description: Developer-facing tools for writing, refactoring, debugging, and shipping code with AI assistance.
children.long_description: Tools that embed LLMs into the software engineering loop: IDE plugins, autonomous SWE harnesses, refactoring assistants, and debuggers. Sibling to Skills and Prompts, which are reusable assets rather than interactive tools. Delivered as CLIs, IDE extensions, or standalone harnesses.

children.name: Prompts
Conversational Control
children.short_description: Frameworks for constraining and steering the conversational behavior of LLM agents via prompt rules.
children.long_description: Tools that define guardrails, flows, and allowed turns for conversational agents at the prompt layer. Distinct from formatting or workflow-definition siblings, which target output shape or task DAGs rather than dialog dynamics. Typically a library or DSL wrapping the model call.

children.name: Feedback Formatting
