children.short_description: Shaping user or tool feedback into prompts so models incorporate it reliably on the next turn.
children.long_description: Utilities that structure critique, evaluation results, or tool errors into a form the LLM can act on. Distinct from general output Formatting (model-to-user) and Structured Output (schema enforcement); this slot is about the feedback signal flowing back into the prompt.

children.name: Formatting
children.short_description: Encoding schemes and token-efficient formats for representing data inside prompts.
children.long_description: Libraries and specs—like TOON—that define compact or LLM-friendly serializations as alternatives to JSON/YAML in prompts. Distinct from Structured Output (enforcing model output shape) and Feedback Formatting (critique). Focused on how input data is rendered for the model.

children.name: Prompt Management
Release & Rollout
children.short_description: Canary, A/B, and staged rollout tooling for prompt and agent config changes.
children.long_description: Release-engineering tools applied to prompts: canary a new prompt to 5% of traffic, roll back on regression, diff live variants. Distinct from version control, which just stores history.

children.short_description: Tools for storing, versioning, and organising prompt libraries across projects and teams.
children.long_description: Systems that treat prompts as first-class artifacts: CRUD, versioning, tagging, and retrieval of prompt templates. Distinct from optimisation (which rewrites prompts) and formatting (which shapes a single prompt). Typically CLIs, web apps, or SDKs backing a prompt store.

children.name: Prompt Optimisation & Compilation
children.short_description: Frameworks that automatically tune, rewrite, or compile prompts from higher-level program specifications.
children.long_description: Libraries that optimise prompts via gradient-like feedback, bootstrapped demonstrations, or programmatic compilation. Distinct from manual prompt management: the prompt is an output, not an input. Usually Python SDKs wrapping an LLM call with a training loop (DSPy, TextGrad, AdalFlow).

children.name: Structured Output
Constrained Decoding Libraries
children.short_description: Libraries that constrain LLM decoding to a grammar, regex, or schema at token level.
children.long_description: Low-level tools that modify sampling to guarantee outputs conform to a formal grammar (JSON Schema, CFG, regex). Examples: Outlines, Guidance, XGrammar, Instructor. Typically Python libraries hooking into local inference runtimes or wrapping provider APIs.

children.short_description: Techniques and libraries for forcing LLMs to emit schema-valid structured data.
children.long_description: Covers constrained decoding, grammar enforcement, and JSON/Pydantic coercion at inference time. Distinct from formatting (stylistic) and workflow definition (control flow). Typically SDKs that wrap model calls and guarantee parseable output.

children.name: Workflow Definition
children.short_description: DSLs and libraries for declaring multi-step prompt workflows and procedures.
children.long_description: Lets authors define the shape of a task — steps, branching, roles — as reusable prompt-driven workflows, separate from full agent orchestration frameworks. Usually a markup, YAML, or code-based DSL consumed by a runner.

children.short_description: Reusable prompt assets and tooling for authoring, managing, formatting, and optimizing LLM prompts.
children.long_description: Prompt engineering surface covering conversational control, formatting, workflow definition, management, structured output, and compiler-style optimizers. Distinct from Skills (packaged capabilities) and Dev Tools (interactive coding tools); this slot is about the prompt as an artifact.

children.name: Skills
Bundles
Claude Code
children.short_description: Skill bundles specifically targeting the Claude Code harness.
children.long_description: Collections of skills authored against Claude Code's skill format and loader conventions. A vendor-specific slot under Bundles. Typically git repos installable into ~/.claude/skills or a plugin marketplace.

children.short_description: Packaged, distributable collections of agent skills shipped as a unit.
children.long_description: Bundles group multiple skills into a single installable artifact (e.g. a Claude Code skill pack). Distinct from registries (discovery) and package libraries (individual modules). Typically tarballs or repo layouts consumed by a skill loader.

children.name: Guides
children.short_description: Instructional skills that teach an agent how to do a task rather than expose code.
children.long_description: Prose/procedure-first skills loaded into context to shape agent behaviour — e.g. a 'taste' guide. Distinct from package libraries (executable modules) and standards (specs). Usually markdown documents consumed by skill-capable harnesses.

children.name: Package Library
children.short_description: Hosted libraries of reusable agent skills exposed as installable packages.
children.long_description: Provider- or platform-hosted catalogues where individual skills can be imported into an agent project (e.g. Vercel Skills). Distinct from registries (indexes across providers) and bundles (grouped packs). Typically an SDK or web console.

children.name: Registries
children.short_description: Indexes and directories for discovering published agent skills across sources.
children.long_description: Browsable listings or APIs that catalogue skills from many authors and platforms. Distinct from package libraries (a single provider's store) and bundles (packaged collections). Typically static sites, JSON indexes, or lightweight APIs.

children.name: Standards
children.short_description: Open specifications defining how agent skills are authored, packaged, and loaded.
children.long_description: Protocol- and schema-level work on what a skill is — its manifest, invocation contract, and interop. Distinct from registries (discovery) and guides (content). Typically a spec document plus reference implementations (e.g. AgentSkills).

children.short_description: Packaged, reusable agent skills—capability bundles with guides, registries, and interoperability standards.
children.long_description: Everything around 'skills' as distributable units of agent capability: bundle formats, package libraries, registries, authoring guides, and cross-vendor standards. Sibling to Prompts (raw text assets) and Dev Tools (interactive IDE tooling). Think plugin ecosystem for agents.

segments.short_description: Tooling, reusable skills, and prompt assets that developers use to build and operate agents.
segments.long_description: Top-level domain covering engineer-facing artifacts: coding assistants and IDE integrations (Dev Tools), packaged agent capabilities (Skills), and prompt engineering assets (Prompts). Excludes runtimes, frameworks, and infra, which live elsewhere in the taxonomy.

segments.name: Ecosystem

children.name: Destinations
children.short_description: Physical and virtual destinations in the agentic AI world — conferences, hubs, venues.
children.long_description: Places people go to engage with the ecosystem: events, meetups, hubs, and community sites. Distinct from organisations (entities) and tools. Acts as a map of where the field congregates.

children.name: Organisations
Companies
children.short_description: For-profit companies operating in the agentic AI space.
children.long_description: Commercial entities building, funding, or selling agentic AI products and services. Distinct from foundations (non-profit) and government bodies. Used to track notable vendors as ecosystem actors, not their individual products.

children.name: Foundations
children.short_description: Non-profit foundations supporting agentic AI standards, research, or community.
children.long_description: Independent non-profits such as the Agentic AI Industry Foundation. Distinct from for-profit companies and multi-party consortia. Tracked as ecosystem stewards rather than tool vendors.

children.name: Foundations & Consortia
children.short_description: Multi-member consortia and joint foundations coordinating agentic AI standards work.
children.long_description: Alliances bringing multiple organisations together around shared specs, protocols, or governance. Distinct from single-entity foundations and private companies. Typically publish specifications and reference implementations.

children.name: Government Bodies
children.short_description: Government and public-sector bodies with roles in agentic AI policy or oversight.
children.long_description: Regulators, agencies, and state-affiliated institutes shaping how agents are deployed and governed. Distinct from private companies and independent foundations. Tracked for their policy and standards influence.

children.short_description: Entities shaping the agentic AI ecosystem: companies, foundations, consortia, and government bodies.
children.long_description: Umbrella for the organisational actors behind standards, funding, and policy. Distinct from destinations (venues) and projects (tools). Subdivided by organisation type.

children.name: Uncategorized
children.short_description: Holding pen for items awaiting classification into the taxonomy.
children.long_description: Temporary bucket for projects or entities not yet assigned a functional slot. Should trend toward empty as the taxonomy is refined. Not a stable category.

segments.short_description: Meta-layer covering non-tool entities in the agentic AI landscape: organisations, destinations, and uncategorised items.
segments.long_description: Holds actors and places around the ecosystem rather than software. Sits outside the functional taxonomy of frameworks, tools, and runtimes. Used for companies, foundations, conferences, and holding pens for items awaiting classification.

segments.name: Frameworks & Runtimes

children.name: Environments
OS
children.short_description: Operating-system-style environments purpose-built as agent hosts, where the OS itself exposes primitives for agent perception and action.
children.long_description: OS-level environments that treat the agent as a first-class citizen — shell, filesystem, and process model shaped around agent use. Distinct from remote desktops (which reuse a standard desktop) in that the OS itself is the abstraction.

children.name: Remote Desktops
children.short_description: Hosted remote desktop environments that agents drive via screen/keyboard/mouse — cloud VMs packaged as agent workspaces.
children.long_description: Remote desktops provisioned for computer-use agents: a real OS GUI streamed over the network that an agent controls. Distinct from OS-level agent environments (custom substrates) — here the desktop is standard and the contribution is the hosting, isolation, and streaming layer.

children.short_description: Execution environments where agents run — operating systems, desktops, and other host surfaces distinct from the framework code itself.
children.long_description: Covers the substrate an agent inhabits: OS-level agent shells, remote desktop hosts, and similar runtime surfaces. Distinct from Runtimes (process/sandbox layer) and Frameworks (programming model); an environment is the place the agent perceives and acts within.

children.name: Frameworks
.NET
children.short_description: .NET-ecosystem agent frameworks — C#/F# libraries for building agents on the .NET runtime.
children.long_description: Agent frameworks targeting the .NET platform, providing idiomatic C#/F# APIs for agents, tools, and memory. Sibling to language/modality-specific framework slots; grouped by host language because ecosystem conventions and package distribution differ meaningfully from Python/TypeScript.

children.name: Computer Use
children.short_description: Frameworks specialized for computer-use agents — primitives for screen observation, GUI control, and desktop task loops.
children.long_description: Libraries whose abstractions are shaped around driving a computer (screenshots, clicks, keystrokes, UI grounding). Distinct from general multi-agent frameworks and from computer-use models themselves; this is the developer-facing scaffolding for building such agents.

children.name: Multiagent Orchestration
children.short_description: Frameworks whose core abstraction is a team of cooperating agents — roles, messaging, and group workflows as first-class primitives.
children.long_description: Libraries built around multi-agent collaboration patterns: role assignment, inter-agent messaging, task delegation. Distinct from the Orchestration segment (standalone coordination services) — these are developer frameworks you import to define the agents and their interactions in code.

children.name: Multimodal
children.short_description: Frameworks for agents that natively handle multiple modalities — video, audio, and vision alongside text.
children.long_description: Agent libraries where multimodal I/O (video, audio, image streams) is a first-class concern rather than an afterthought. Distinct from voice-only or text-only frameworks; typical interface is an SDK with streaming media primitives wired into the agent loop.

children.name: Optimisation
Parallelisation
children.short_description: Framework-level optimisation techniques for running agent workloads in parallel to cut latency or cost.
children.long_description: Parallelisation primitives inside agent frameworks — concurrent tool calls, fan-out/fan-in execution, parallel chain steps. Complements other optimisation strategies (batching, caching). Exposed as framework APIs or execution planners.

children.short_description: Frameworks focused on optimising agent execution — parallelisation, batching, and runtime efficiency of agent workloads.
children.long_description: Libraries whose contribution is making agent systems faster or cheaper to run: parallel execution of sub-tasks, batched tool calls, scheduling tricks. Distinct from Orchestration (routing) — optimisation frameworks shape how individual agent workloads execute, not how multiple agents coordinate.

children.name: Social Simulations
children.short_description: Frameworks for simulating populations of agents in social settings — generative agents, games, and behavioral experiments.
children.long_description: Libraries designed to instantiate many interacting agents for sociological, economic, or behavioral simulation. Distinct from multi-agent orchestration (which targets task completion): the deliverable is the simulated society itself, often for research or game design.

children.name: Stateful Design
children.short_description: Frameworks that treat persistent agent state and memory as the central design concern — long-lived agents with durable identity.
children.long_description: Libraries organized around stateful agents: memory hierarchies, self-editing context, and long-term persistence are first-class. Distinct from stateless chain-of-tool frameworks; typical interface is a server plus SDK where agents outlive a single request and accumulate memory across sessions.

children.name: Voice
Realtime Voice Pipelines
children.short_description: Frameworks wiring STT, LLM, and TTS into low-latency duplex pipelines for conversational voice agents.
children.long_description: Toolkits for building realtime voice agents that stream audio in and out with interruption handling and turn-taking. Distinct from generic voice SDKs by orchestrating the full pipeline. Typically Python/Node libraries or managed services.

children.name: Telephony Agents
children.short_description: Platforms for building agents that place and receive phone calls over SIP/PSTN.
children.long_description: Voice-agent platforms whose primary surface is the telephone network — inbound IVR replacement, outbound calling, SIP trunking, carrier integration. Distinct from Realtime Voice Pipelines (which is about the STT→LLM→TTS loop in-browser or in-app); telephony adds call control, DTMF, warm transfer, and carrier-grade latency constraints.

children.short_description: Voice-first agent frameworks — libraries built around realtime speech I/O, turn-taking, and telephony-style interaction.
children.long_description: Frameworks whose primary modality is voice: streaming STT/TTS, barge-in, realtime pipelines, and telephony integration. Distinct from generic multimodal frameworks by their realtime-voice focus. Typical interface: an SDK plus a media server coordinating audio streams with LLM turns.

children.name: Workflow Orchestration
Batch & Bulk Agents
children.short_description: Frameworks for running the same agent across large batches of inputs.
children.long_description: Batch-flavoured frameworks optimised for sweeping an agent over thousands or millions of rows — cost control, partial-failure handling, and resumable progress matter more than latency or memory.

children.name: DAG Pipelines
children.short_description: LLM-aware DAG frameworks for data-flow style agentic pipelines.
children.long_description: Pipeline frameworks where steps form a directed acyclic graph and the runtime executes them in topological order. LLM calls are first-class nodes alongside transforms, branches, and joins. Suited to document processing, enrichment, and ETL-flavoured agent work.

children.name: Durable Agent Workflows
children.short_description: Agent-framing atop durable workflow engines, with resumable, exactly-once steps.
children.long_description: The framework layer sitting on top of durable execution engines (Temporal, Restate, Inngest, DBOS) that exposes an agent-authoring API. Distinct from the engines themselves, which live under Runtimes/Durable Execution.

children.name: ETL & Enrichment
children.short_description: Agentic ETL frameworks where LLM steps classify, extract, and enrich row-oriented data.
children.long_description: Frameworks where the workflow is fundamentally a data transformation, with LLMs standing in for regex, rules, or classifiers. Distinct from general pipelines because the shape is explicitly tabular/row-at-a-time.

children.name: Event-Driven Workflows
children.short_description: Workflows triggered and advanced by external events rather than user messages.
children.long_description: Workflows whose progression is driven by webhooks, queue messages, file drops, or schedules rather than a conversational turn. The agent is a participant in an event mesh, not a chat partner.

children.name: Human-Gated Workflows
children.short_description: Long-running workflows that pause at defined checkpoints for human input before resuming.
children.long_description: Workflow frameworks with first-class suspend-until-human primitives — the workflow parks, emits a review task, and resumes when a human responds. Distinct from Human in the Loop interaction patterns (which are runtime behaviours) — this slot is the framework-level primitive.

children.name: Scheduled & Recurring Workflows
children.short_description: Cron-style agentic workflows that run on a schedule without human initiation.
children.long_description: Scheduled execution of agentic pipelines — nightly enrichment runs, weekly reports, periodic reconciliation. The framework's job is to manage schedule, idempotency, and catch-up on missed runs.

children.name: State Machine Workflows
children.short_description: Explicit state-machine frameworks for agentic workflows with branching and loops.
children.long_description: Frameworks that model the workflow as a finite state machine with guarded transitions — appropriate when control flow has real branches, loops, and recovery states rather than a linear DAG.

children.short_description: Frameworks for building persistent, non-conversational agentic workflows and pipelines.
children.long_description: Frameworks whose primary unit is a workflow or pipeline — a DAG or state machine of steps that may invoke LLMs — rather than a conversation. Used for back-office automation, data pipelines, and long-running business processes where the agent is one component among many. Distinct from Orchestration under Runtimes (which focuses on agent-to-agent coordination at runtime) and from Stateful Design (which focuses on conversational state).

children.short_description: Programming-model libraries and SDKs for building agents — the code-facing abstractions developers import to define agent behavior.
children.long_description: Libraries, SDKs, and DSLs that provide the primitives (agents, tools, memory, loops) for authoring agentic systems. Distinct from Runtimes (execution), Orchestration (multi-agent coordination services), and Harnesses (wrappers around existing agents). Subdivided by language, modality, and design style.

children.name: Harnesses
Checkpointing
children.short_description: Harness features that snapshot agent state mid-run so tasks can be paused, resumed, or branched without replaying from scratch.
children.long_description: Mechanisms for capturing and restoring an agent's working state — memory, scratchpad, tool history — at arbitrary points in a long task. Distinct from retry/recovery (which handles failure) and context compression (which shrinks state); checkpointing is about durable resumability.

children.name: Context Compression
children.short_description: Harness techniques that shrink an agent's growing context window — summarisation, pruning, and hierarchical memory for long runs.
children.long_description: Strategies and tools for keeping context usable as an agent accumulates history: rolling summaries, selective eviction, hierarchical notes. Distinct from general memory systems — this sits inside the agent loop to keep a single run within budget, rather than providing cross-session memory.

children.name: Deep Research
children.short_description: Harnesses specialised for long-horizon, multi-step research tasks with iterative search, reading, and synthesis.
children.long_description: Agent scaffolds that drive deep-research workflows: planning queries, fanning out to retrieval, reading sources, and composing reports. Distinct from generic sub-agent dispatch in that the harness is opinionated around research loops. Typically a library or runtime template layered over an LLM.

children.name: Platform Specific
macOS
children.short_description: Agent harnesses built specifically for macOS, leveraging native APIs and Apple silicon integration.
children.long_description: Harnesses that target macOS as a first-class host — using AppKit, Accessibility, or Metal/MLX for local inference and control. Shipped as native apps or Mac-only binaries. Sibling slot to other OS-specific harnesses under Platform Specific.

children.short_description: Agent harnesses tightly coupled to a specific host OS or platform rather than being runtime-agnostic.
children.long_description: Harnesses whose design assumes a particular platform (e.g. macOS, iOS, Windows) for native APIs, sandboxing, or UI control. In contrast to cross-platform harnesses, these trade portability for deep platform integration. Usually shipped as native apps or platform SDKs.

children.name: Retry and Recovery
children.short_description: Harness components handling failure detection, retry policy, and recovery from tool or model errors mid-task.
children.long_description: Mechanisms that let an agent survive transient tool failures, malformed outputs, or stalled reasoning — backoff, re-planning, fallback paths. Sibling to checkpointing (which persists state) but focused on in-loop error handling. Exposed as harness middleware or runtime policies.

children.name: Sub-agent Dispatch
children.short_description: Harness logic for spawning, routing to, and collecting results from sub-agents under a parent controller.
children.long_description: Covers the dispatch layer that lets a primary agent delegate work to specialised sub-agents and merge their outputs. Distinct from general multi-agent orchestration in that it lives inside a single harness as a control primitive. Usually a library API for task fan-out.

children.name: Task Coherence
children.short_description: Harness features that keep an agent on-task across long runs, preventing drift and goal decay.
children.long_description: Techniques for maintaining alignment between an agent's current actions and its original goal — goal reminders, plan re-grounding, scope guards. Complements context compression (which shrinks history) by ensuring intent survives the compression. Typically library-level scaffolding.

children.short_description: Wrappers that extend or control an existing agent/LLM loop — adding research, dispatch, checkpointing, recovery, or compression around it.
children.long_description: Harnesses sit around a base agent or model and add cross-cutting behaviors: long-horizon research, sub-agent dispatch, context compression, retry and recovery, checkpointing. Distinct from Frameworks (you build with) and Runtimes (you run on); a harness is what you bolt onto a model or agent to make it durable and task-coherent.

children.name: Loops
children.short_description: Minimal agent loop patterns — tight read-think-act cycles exposed as the primary abstraction rather than a full framework.
children.long_description: Projects whose central contribution is the iteration loop itself: a small, opinionated control flow that repeatedly prompts, acts, and observes. Distinct from Frameworks (which bundle many concerns) and Harnesses (which wrap existing agents). Useful as building blocks or reference implementations.

children.name: Orchestration
Platform-Specific
children.short_description: Orchestration frameworks tied to a specific cloud, vendor, or platform ecosystem.
children.long_description: Multi-agent orchestration offerings that assume a particular provider (e.g. AWS, Azure) for routing, state, and tool integration. Distinct from vendor-neutral orchestrators. Usually delivered as SDKs or managed services bound to that platform's primitives.

children.short_description: Coordination layers that route tasks across multiple agents, models, or workers — the conductor rather than the individual performer.
children.long_description: Services and libraries that dispatch, schedule, and compose work across many agents or model endpoints. Distinct from multi-agent frameworks (which define the agents) in that orchestration focuses on routing, policy, and cross-agent flow. Typical interface: a controller service or SDK that agents and tools register with.

children.name: Pipelines
children.short_description: Declarative, staged data/agent pipelines — DAG-style flows that transform inputs into agent-driven outputs.
children.long_description: Pipeline-oriented tooling for chaining extractors, transformers, and agent stages into a repeatable flow. Distinct from Orchestration (dynamic multi-agent routing) and Loops (tight iteration): pipelines emphasize staged, mostly linear transformations, often to construct agents or artifacts from source material.

children.name: Runtimes
Data Layers
children.short_description: Runtime-adjacent storage and data-access layers that agents rely on for state, memory, and dataset I/O.
children.long_description: Data substrates embedded in or paired with agent runtimes — versioned datasets, vector-ready lakes, append-only logs. Sibling to sandboxes (execution) and policy engines (authorisation). Usually a library or service exposing a dataset or storage API to running agents.

children.name: Durable Execution
children.short_description: Workflow engines that give agents durable, resumable execution across failures and restarts.
children.long_description: Durable workflow runtimes (originating in backend workflow orchestration) repurposed or marketed for agent execution — promises of exactly-once steps, automatic retry, replay, and long-running state. Distinct from general Orchestration because the guarantee is durability, not just composition.

children.name: Event & Messaging Substrates
children.short_description: Message buses and event streams that carry inter-agent and agent-to-tool traffic.
children.long_description: Pub/sub brokers, streaming logs, and event meshes used as the transport layer between agents, tools, and humans. Distinct from A2A (which specifies the protocol) — this slot is the substrate underneath.

children.name: Policy Engines
children.short_description: Runtime components that evaluate and enforce permission, safety, or governance policies on agent actions.
children.long_description: Authorisation and guardrail engines invoked by an agent runtime before tool calls or state mutations. Distinct from data layers and sandboxes in that they answer allow/deny questions against declared rules. Typically embedded libraries or sidecar services.

children.name: Sandboxes
Code Execution Sandboxes
children.short_description: Sandboxes purpose-built for running LLM-generated code with fast startup and per-session isolation.
children.long_description: Managed or self-hosted services that spin up disposable VMs/containers for each agent code-execution request, with filesystem, network, and package tooling. Distinct from general sandboxes by optimising for code interpreters. Consumed via SDKs and REST APIs.

children.short_description: Isolated execution environments where agents can run code, shell commands, or untrusted tools safely.
children.long_description: Ephemeral, isolated compute for agent-driven actions, ranging from microVMs to container-based sandboxes. Distinct from policy engines (rules) and data layers (state) by providing an execution surface. Delivered as APIs, services, or embeddable runtimes.

children.short_description: Execution substrates that host agent processes — sandboxes, policy engines, and data layers that enforce isolation and lifecycle.
children.long_description: Runtime layers providing the OS-like concerns for agents: sandboxing, policy enforcement, persistent data. Distinct from Frameworks (developer API) and Environments (the surface the agent acts on); the runtime is what actually runs the agent process and governs what it can touch.

children.name: Starter Kits
Social Simulations
children.short_description: Starter kits for building multi-agent social simulations with personas, worlds, and interactions.
children.long_description: Opinionated templates for populating a simulated environment with many agents that socialise, plan, and act — à la generative agents research. Distinct from general multi-agent orchestration by providing world, persona, and UI scaffolding. Usually a runnable sample project.

children.short_description: Opinionated scaffolds and templates for bootstrapping a new agent project — boilerplate plus conventions, not a framework.
children.long_description: Ready-to-clone templates, reference apps, and scaffolds that get a developer from zero to a running agent quickly. Distinct from Frameworks (the library they wrap) in that a starter kit is a project skeleton, often specialized by domain (e.g. social simulations).

segments.short_description: Top-level domain for the software substrate that executes agents: frameworks, runtimes, harnesses, loops, and orchestration.
segments.long_description: Covers everything an agent runs on or is built with — from authoring frameworks and step loops to orchestration engines, runtimes, and starter kits. Distinct from tools (capabilities the agent calls) and models. The plumbing layer of the stack.

segments.name: Interfaces

children.name: Annotation
Human Labelling Services
children.short_description: Managed human-in-the-loop labelling and RLHF services for agent training.
children.long_description: Vendor services that supply trained human labellers for agent trajectories, preference data, and red-teaming runs. Distinct from Annotation tools (software) — this slot is the service layer.

children.short_description: Tools for labelling, annotating, and reviewing agent data — traces, outputs, training examples.
children.long_description: Interfaces where humans add labels, corrections, or judgements to agent inputs and outputs. Used for dataset curation, eval scoring, and RLHF-style feedback. Distinct from workspaces and frontends by being editorial rather than conversational.

children.name: Control Surfaces
Agent Cockpits
children.short_description: Single-agent operator views showing live state, plan, and step-by-step reasoning.
children.long_description: Cockpit-style surfaces focused on one agent at a time — the live plan, current tool call, memory state, and controls to pause, edit, or redirect it.

children.name: Agent Inboxes
children.short_description: Inbox-style products where humans triage, approve, or reply to agent actions.
children.long_description: Dedicated inbox applications that collect items requiring human attention from one or many agents — approvals, clarifications, failures — and present them as a reviewable queue.

children.name: Fleet Dashboards
children.short_description: Dashboards that show the state of many agents at once.
children.long_description: Operator views onto populations of running agents — status, throughput, cost, failures — typically used by teams running agent fleets at scale.

children.name: Intervention Consoles
children.short_description: Consoles that let an operator interrupt a running agent and take manual control.
children.long_description: Products whose primary purpose is giving humans a fast path to break into a running session — pause, rewind, edit state, or take over execution. Adjacent to Human in the Loop / Interrupt & Takeover, which names the *pattern*.

children.name: Policy & Governance Consoles
children.short_description: Admin surfaces for defining policies, permissions, and budgets that agents must obey.
children.long_description: Governance-oriented control planes where operators configure what agents may do, which tools they may call, cost ceilings, and who may authorise exceptions. Distinct from runtime guardrails because the surface is the policy editor, not the enforcement point.

children.short_description: Operator-facing surfaces for supervising, steering, and intervening in running agents.
children.long_description: Dashboards, inboxes, cockpits, and consoles through which humans manage agents in flight — distinct from Frontends (end-user chat) and from UX primitives (reusable widgets). The unit of interaction is an agent or fleet rather than a conversation. Overlaps with Safety & Governance / Human in the Loop (which classifies the *interaction patterns*); Control Surfaces classifies the *products* that implement them.

children.name: Frontends
Android
children.short_description: Android-native client apps for interacting with agents and LLMs on mobile.
children.long_description: Frontends packaged as Android applications, using native OS capabilities for notifications, background tasks, and device integration. Distinct from cross-platform mobile clients by targeting Android specifically. Distributed via APK or Play Store.

children.name: Chat UIs
children.short_description: Chat-style frontends that render agent conversations as threaded message UIs.
children.long_description: Reusable chat interface components and apps designed specifically for agent interactions — message streams, tool-call rendering, and thread history. Distinct from platform-specific desktop/mobile/web clients: focuses on the chat UX paradigm itself, often as an embeddable library or shell over an underlying agent runtime.

children.name: Desktop Clients
Cross-Platform
children.short_description: Desktop agent clients built to run on Windows, macOS, and Linux from a single codebase.
children.long_description: Electron/Tauri/Qt-style desktop apps targeting all three major OSes. Distinguished from per-platform siblings by a shared codebase and unified feature parity across operating systems.

children.name: Linux
children.short_description: Linux-native or Linux-first desktop clients for agents.
children.long_description: Desktop agent applications specifically packaged for Linux — often via Flatpak, AppImage, or distro repos — or taking advantage of Linux-specific integrations. Distinct from cross-platform builds by being Linux-targeted primary releases.

children.name: macOS
children.short_description: macOS-native desktop clients for agents.
children.long_description: Agent desktop apps built for macOS, often leveraging Apple frameworks and shipped via DMG or the App Store. Distinguished from cross-platform builds by being Mac-only or Mac-first.

children.name: Windows
children.short_description: Windows-native desktop clients for agents.
children.long_description: Agent desktop apps distributed as Windows installers (MSI/EXE/Store) with Windows-specific integrations. Separated from cross-platform builds to capture tools that are Windows-only or Windows-first.

children.short_description: Native or packaged desktop applications for interacting with agents on Windows, macOS, or Linux.
children.long_description: Installable desktop apps exposing agent chat, tools, and workflows through an OS-native shell. Unlike web clients, they run locally with filesystem and OS integration; subdivided by target platform (Windows, macOS, Linux, Cross-Platform).

children.name: GUIs and CLIs
children.short_description: Hybrid frontends exposing the same agent through both a graphical interface and a command-line entry point.
children.long_description: Tools that ship a GUI alongside a CLI, letting users drive the same agent interactively or from scripts. Distinct from pure TUI/terminal clients: here the GUI is a first-class surface, not an afterthought.

children.name: Mobile Clients
Android
children.short_description: Android apps for interacting with agents.
children.long_description: Agent clients packaged as APKs/AABs for Android phones and tablets, distributed via Play Store or sideload. Distinct from iOS siblings by platform SDK, distribution, and integration surface.

children.name: iOS
children.short_description: iOS apps for interacting with agents on iPhone and iPad.
children.long_description: Agent clients built with Apple's iOS SDKs and distributed via the App Store or TestFlight. Separated from Android siblings by platform, distribution, and sandboxing model.

children.short_description: Mobile apps (iOS/Android) for interacting with agents from phones and tablets.
children.long_description: Native or hybrid mobile applications exposing agent chat and tools on handheld devices. Separated from desktop and web clients because mobile constraints (touch, push, background) shape the UX; subdivided by platform.

children.name: Server Software
children.short_description: Self-hostable server backends that power agent frontends with multi-user state, auth, and persistence.
children.long_description: Backend services that sit behind chat/web/desktop clients, providing accounts, conversation storage, model routing, and tool access. Unlike inference servers, their job is frontend-facing session and UX state, not raw model execution.

children.name: Terminal Clients
children.short_description: Terminal-resident clients that expose agents as a shell-style REPL or command.
children.long_description: Lightweight terminal programs for chatting with or dispatching agents from the command line. Simpler than full TUIs (no panels/keybindings) and distinct from GUI/desktop clients — the terminal is the entire surface.

children.name: TUIs and CLIs
children.short_description: Text-based interactive frontends combining terminal UIs with command-line invocation.
children.long_description: Interactive TUI dashboards plus scriptable CLI entry points for agents, running fully inside a terminal. Distinct from pure terminal clients by offering richer panel/keybinding UIs, and from GUI+CLI hybrids by staying text-only.

children.name: Web Clients
children.short_description: Browser-based frontends for agents, served as web apps or SPAs.
children.long_description: Hosted or self-hostable web applications providing chat and tool UIs to agents over HTTP. Distinct from desktop clients (no install) and from server software (which powers them): this slot is the browser-facing UI layer.

children.short_description: Client applications that expose agent capabilities to end users across desktop, mobile, web, and terminal.
children.long_description: End-user client software — chat apps, desktop clients, mobile apps, TUIs, web UIs — that front an agent backend. Distinct from workspaces (multi-agent environments) and UX primitives. Usually a shipped binary or web app talking to model/agent APIs.

children.name: UX
Human in the Loop
children.short_description: UX patterns and inboxes letting humans review, approve, or correct agent actions mid-run.
children.long_description: Tools providing approval queues, intervention points, and review inboxes where an agent pauses for human judgment before proceeding. Typically integrates with an orchestrator via webhooks or SDK callbacks; distinct from generic chat UIs by centering on gating and oversight workflows.

children.short_description: Reusable UX patterns and primitives for agent interaction, such as human-in-the-loop approvals and inspection surfaces.
children.long_description: Design patterns and component libraries that shape how users supervise, approve, or correct agent actions. Distinct from full frontends by being pattern-level rather than application-level. Often delivered as UI libraries or reference implementations.

children.name: Workspaces
children.short_description: Multi-agent workspace environments where users manage, converse with, and orchestrate several agents in one place.
children.long_description: Integrated environments that host multiple agents, conversations, and tools behind a unified UI — closer to an IDE or team workspace than a single chat client. Distinct from frontends (single-client) by being multi-agent and multi-session. Typically web apps or desktop workspaces.

segments.short_description: User-facing surfaces through which humans interact with agents — frontends, workspaces, UX patterns, and annotation tools.
segments.long_description: Top-level domain covering everything a human sees or touches when working with agents: client apps, chat UIs, workspace environments, UX primitives like human-in-the-loop, and data annotation interfaces. Sits above the runtime and framework layers.

segments.name: Models & Inference

children.name: Inference
Gateways
Context Compression
children.short_description: Gateway-layer services that compress or prune context windows before requests reach the model.
children.long_description: Middleware that shrinks prompts — via summarization, dedup, or token pruning — to fit budgets and cut cost. Deployed as an inline gateway component. Distinct from memory stores and from model-side KV compression.

children.short_description: Unified API gateways that front multiple model providers behind a single endpoint.
children.long_description: Services exposing one OpenAI-compatible (or similar) API that fans out to many providers, applying auth, quota, logging, and context compression. Distinct from routers (which pick a model per request) and proxies (which are thinner pass-throughs) by offering a full management plane.

children.name: Inference Servers
children.short_description: Servers that execute model inference locally or on dedicated hardware.
children.long_description: Runtimes such as vLLM-style engines that load model weights and expose an HTTP/gRPC inference API. Distinct from gateways/routers (which forward to other backends) — inference servers actually run the model and generate tokens.

children.name: Proxy
Gateways
children.short_description: Unified LLM gateway proxies exposing many providers behind one API with auth, logging, and rate limiting.
children.long_description: Covers multi-provider proxies like LiteLLM and AgentGateway that normalize provider APIs and add ops features. Sit in front of all model traffic for an agent or org. Distinct from pure routers (decision logic) and protocol bridges.

children.name: MCP to OpenAI
children.short_description: Bridges that expose MCP servers as OpenAI-compatible tool endpoints.
children.long_description: Proxies such as MCPO that translate between the Model Context Protocol and the OpenAI tool/function-calling API, letting OpenAI-style clients consume MCP tools. Distinct from generic LLM gateways.

children.short_description: Transparent proxies that sit between agents and model APIs, forwarding requests while adapting protocols or endpoints.
children.long_description: Covers proxy-style middleware for LLM traffic: protocol bridging (e.g. MCP to OpenAI), endpoint translation, and drop-in API compatibility layers. Distinct from Gateways (policy/auth), Routers (model selection), and Inference Servers (actual execution).

children.name: Routers
Cost Optimisation
children.short_description: Routers whose primary objective is minimising spend by dispatching to the cheapest adequate model or provider.
children.long_description: Cost-aware routing layers that track per-token pricing and redirect traffic to cheaper backends when quality allows. Distinct from intelligent (capability-based) and tool-specific routers.

children.name: Intelligent
children.short_description: Capability-aware routers that classify prompts and dispatch to the best-suited model.
children.long_description: Semantic or learned routers (Semantic Router, Agentic Flow) that inspect intent or difficulty and pick a model accordingly. Distinct from cost-first and tool-specific routing.

children.name: Tool Specific
children.short_description: Routers specialised for dispatching tool calls to the model best suited to execute them.
children.long_description: Routing layers that look at the tool-use surface of a request and pick a model with strong performance for that tool or schema. Distinct from general capability or cost routers.

children.short_description: Request routers that pick which model or provider should handle a given prompt based on cost, capability, or tool affinity.
children.long_description: Libraries and services that decide, per-request, which backend LLM to call. Typically exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint and dispatches based on heuristics, classifiers, or policies. Distinct from Gateways (auth/observability) and Proxies (protocol translation).

children.name: Semantic Caching
children.short_description: Caches that reuse prior LLM responses when a new prompt is semantically similar to one already seen.
children.long_description: Middleware that embeds prompts and returns cached completions on vector-similarity hits, cutting cost and latency. Usually deployed as a proxy or SDK in front of the model. Distinct from generic HTTP caches and from KV-cache reuse inside inference servers.

children.name: Structured Output Gateways
children.short_description: Gateways that enforce typed, schema-valid outputs from LLMs via constrained decoding, grammars, or retry loops.
children.long_description: Tools like BAML and TensorZero that wrap model calls to guarantee JSON/DSL-conformant responses, handling parsing, validation, and repair. Sit between the agent and the raw inference endpoint. Distinct from routing or caching gateways focused on dispatch or reuse.

children.short_description: Infrastructure that serves, routes, and optimises model calls between agents and model backends.
children.long_description: The serving plane: inference servers, gateways, proxies, routers, semantic caches, and structured-output enforcement that sit between an agent and the underlying model. Distinct from the Models sibling, which covers the models themselves rather than how requests reach them.

children.name: Models
Distillation
children.short_description: Tools for distilling large models into smaller student models for agent use.
children.long_description: Training-time techniques and toolkits for producing a smaller model that mimics a larger one — useful for agents that need low-latency, low-cost inference in tight loops.

children.name: Embedding Models
children.short_description: Embedding models that turn text (or other modalities) into vectors for retrieval, clustering, and semantic search.
children.long_description: Covers released embedding models used by agents for RAG, memory, and similarity search. Distinct from LLMs (generative) and from the databases or pipelines that consume the vectors.

children.name: LLMs
Agent Specific
children.short_description: LLMs purpose-built or tuned for agentic workflows — planning, tool use, multi-agent coordination.
children.long_description: Models released with agent scenarios as the primary target, e.g. multi-agent variants of frontier models. Distinct from general-purpose LLMs grouped by use case.

children.name: By Use Case
Computer Use
children.short_description: LLMs tuned specifically for computer-use agents that control a GUI, browser, or desktop.
children.long_description: Models such as Fara released for screen-grounded action prediction and desktop/browser automation. Distinct from general vision-language models and from the harnesses that execute the actions.

children.short_description: LLMs grouped by the specific downstream use case they are optimised for.
children.long_description: Parent slot for LLMs released or tuned for particular task families (computer use, coding, etc.). Distinct from agent-specific models where agency itself is the target.

children.short_description: Generative large language models used as the reasoning core of agents.
children.long_description: Frontier and open LLM releases positioned for agentic use, including variants tuned for specific use cases or agent workflows. Distinct from embedding models, RL policies, and the tooling that trains or serves them.

children.name: Quantisation
children.short_description: Tools and formats for compressing models so they run on smaller hardware.
children.long_description: Quantisation libraries and file formats — GGUF, AWQ, GPTQ, bitsandbytes, llm-compressor — that let the same model run on constrained hardware at the cost of some accuracy. Distinct from Inference Servers, which consume quantised weights.

children.name: Reinforcement Learning
Agent RL Environments
children.short_description: Environment suites and harnesses for training and evaluating agents via reinforcement learning.
children.long_description: Projects like SkyRL, Verifiers, and OpenEnv that provide standardized agent environments, reward signals, and rollout infrastructure. Distinct from RL algorithms themselves and from eval benchmarks used purely for scoring.

children.short_description: RL frameworks, algorithms, and environments used to train or post-train agentic models.
children.long_description: Covers RL libraries (Acme-style) and agent-focused RL runtimes (RLinf), plus environment suites for agent training. Distinct from supervised trainers and from the models themselves; sits upstream of deployed agents.

children.name: Speech Models
Speaker Diarisation
children.short_description: Who-spoke-when segmentation of multi-speaker audio.
children.long_description: Diarisation models that assign speaker labels to audio segments — needed by agents that process meetings, calls, or multi-party conversations.

children.name: Speech-to-Text
children.short_description: ASR models used by agents to transcribe user speech.
children.long_description: Automatic speech recognition models — open-weights and hosted APIs — that convert audio into text for downstream agent reasoning. Latency and streaming support matter more than raw WER for agentic use.

children.name: Text-to-Speech
children.short_description: TTS models used by agents to synthesise spoken responses.
children.long_description: Neural text-to-speech models that agents use to respond with voice. Distinguished by streaming latency, voice cloning, and prosody control rather than pure naturalness.

children.name: Voice Activity Detection
children.short_description: Detectors that decide when a user has started or stopped speaking.
children.long_description: VAD models and libraries used in voice-agent pipelines to segment speech from silence, drive turn-taking, and trigger STT. A prerequisite for low-latency voice agents.

children.short_description: Speech-to-text and text-to-speech models that agents depend on for voice I/O.
children.long_description: Open and hosted speech models used as the ears and mouth of voice agents. Covers ASR/STT (transcription), TTS (synthesis), and end-to-end speech models. Distinct from Frameworks/Voice (which orchestrates the loop) and from Telephony Agents (which handle the phone transport).

children.name: Synthetic Data
children.short_description: Pipelines and tools for generating synthetic training or eval data for agent models.
children.long_description: Covers projects that produce LLM-generated datasets — instructions, trajectories, tool-use traces — used to fine-tune or evaluate agents. Distinct from trainers (which consume the data) and from eval frameworks (which score models).

children.name: Trainers
Tool-Use Fine-Tuning
children.short_description: Fine-tuning services and pipelines specialised in teaching models to call tools and APIs correctly.
children.long_description: Covers OpenPipe, Together fine-tuning, and similar offerings focused on tool-call trajectories and function-calling accuracy. Distinct from generic SFT trainers and from RL environments.

children.short_description: Training and fine-tuning frameworks aimed at producing agent-capable models.
children.long_description: Libraries and services for SFT, DPO, and agent-specific fine-tuning (e.g. tool-use). Covers end-to-end trainers like Agent Lightning. Distinct from RL frameworks and from hosted inference; sits between raw data and deployable checkpoints.

children.short_description: The models themselves and tools for producing them — LLMs, embeddings, trainers, RL, and synthetic data.
children.long_description: Covers base and fine-tuned models an agent consumes (LLMs, embedding models) plus the pipelines that create them (trainers, RL, synthetic data generation). Sibling of Inference, which handles serving; this slot is about what gets served.

segments.short_description: The model layer: base models themselves and the infrastructure that serves them to agents.
segments.long_description: Top-level segment covering both the models an agent calls (LLMs, embeddings, trainers, RL, synthetic data) and the inference plumbing around them (servers, gateways, routers, caches). Distinct from frameworks and tooling: this is where tokens are generated and routed, not where agent logic is authored.

segments.name: Observability & Evals

children.name: Agent Analytics
children.short_description: Product analytics tailored to agent behavior — task success, tool usage, session funnels, user interactions.
children.long_description: Dashboards and metrics pipelines that treat agent runs as the unit of analysis, surfacing trends in completion rates, tool calls, latency, and drop-off. Distinct from raw observability (traces) and evals (scored runs): the focus is aggregate product/usage insight over time.

children.name: Benchmarks
General Assistant Benchmarks
children.short_description: Benchmarks measuring broad, open-ended assistant capability across mixed reasoning, tool-use, and knowledge tasks.
children.long_description: Task suites like GAIA and AgentBench that probe general-purpose agent competence rather than a narrow vertical. Typically scored end-to-end on diverse real-world questions. Sibling to SWE and web-agent benchmarks, which focus on specific domains.

children.name: Software Engineering Benchmarks
children.short_description: Benchmarks grading agents on code tasks — bug fixing, PR resolution, terminal workflows, freelance-style tickets.
children.long_description: Includes SWE-bench-style repo tasks, terminal-based shell tasks, and freelance/contract coding challenges. Typical interface: a dockerized repo plus test suite used as pass/fail oracle. Distinct from web-agent and general-assistant benchmarks.

children.name: Web Agent Benchmarks
children.short_description: Benchmarks that evaluate agents on browser and web navigation tasks in realistic or simulated sites.
children.long_description: Harnesses like WebArena, VisualWebArena and Mind2Web that score agents on multi-step tasks across reproducible web environments. Typical interface: a sandboxed web stack plus task specs. Distinct from OS-level computer-use benchmarks and SWE benchmarks.

children.short_description: Standardized task suites and datasets used to score agent or LLM capability under controlled conditions.
children.long_description: Parent slot for benchmark harnesses and datasets — software engineering, web navigation, general assistants, and more. Typical interface: a task runner plus grading script that produces a comparable score. Distinct from eval frameworks, which build custom in-house test sets.

children.name: Evals and Experiment Tracking
Agent Testing
children.short_description: Unit and integration testing frameworks for agents, modelled on software testing.
children.long_description: Testing frameworks where the unit under test is an agent or tool call — assertions over tool-call sequences, fixture-based scenarios, deterministic replay, CI-friendly runners. Distinct from Evals (which score quality) — testing is about regressions and correctness.

children.name: Frameworks
children.short_description: General-purpose eval frameworks for authoring, running, and scoring test suites against LLM and agent outputs.
children.long_description: Libraries such as DeepEval that provide assertions, metrics, and dataset runners for LLM/agent testing. Typical interface: pytest-like SDK or CLI. Distinct from LLM-as-judge frameworks (judge-specific) and observability (trace-centric).

children.name: LLM-as-Judge Frameworks
children.short_description: Frameworks that use an LLM to grade outputs of another LLM or agent against rubrics or reference answers.
children.long_description: Tools like Promptfoo, Inspect AI, and Patronus that orchestrate judge prompts, rubrics, and scoring pipelines. Typical interface: YAML/DSL tests plus a judge model. Distinct from generic eval frameworks by centering on model-graded scoring.

children.name: Observability
Telemetry Standards
children.short_description: Open standards for agent and LLM telemetry — spans, traces, and semantic conventions.
children.long_description: Specs that define how agent runs are instrumented for tracing and metrics in a vendor-neutral way. Distinct from the Observability products themselves, which implement the standards.

children.short_description: Tracing and telemetry backends that capture LLM/agent spans, prompts, and tool calls for live debugging.
children.long_description: Services like Langfuse that ingest structured traces from SDK instrumentation and expose a UI for run inspection, filtering, and drill-down. Distinct from eval frameworks (scoring) and time-travel debuggers (replay).

children.name: Synthetic User Simulation

examples.name: Hugging Face Agents Course user sims

children.short_description: Tools that generate simulated users to exercise agents in multi-turn conversational or task scenarios.
children.long_description: Harnesses such as Tau-bench and Snowglobe that script or LLM-drive fake users for stress testing, regression, and red-teaming of conversational agents. Distinct from static benchmarks by producing dynamic, interactive dialogues.

children.name: Trace Replay & Time-Travel Debugging
children.short_description: Tools that replay captured agent traces step-by-step, letting developers branch, rewind, and re-run from any point.
children.long_description: Services like Braintrust, AgentOps Replay, and LangSmith that turn stored runs into interactive debug sessions — edit a prompt mid-trace and re-execute. Distinct from passive observability by enabling intervention and what-if replay.

children.short_description: Frameworks and services for running evals, tracing agent runs, and tracking experiments across model/prompt versions.
children.long_description: Covers eval harnesses, LLM-as-judge runners, observability/tracing backends, time-travel debugging, and synthetic user simulators. Typical interface: SDKs that log runs plus a UI for comparison. Sits above raw logging and below product analytics.

children.name: Operations
Cost Monitoring
children.short_description: Tracking token spend, API costs, and budget alerts across models, agents, and tenants.
children.long_description: Tools like AgentOps that attribute LLM/API cost to runs, users, or features and raise alerts on budget breaches. Typical interface: SDK hooks plus a billing dashboard. Distinct from general observability by centering on dollars and quotas.

children.short_description: Day-two operational tooling for running agent and LLM systems in production — cost, budgets, SLOs.
children.long_description: Ops-facing concerns beyond eval quality: spend tracking, rate-limit management, SLO monitoring, on-call signals. Currently anchored by cost monitoring. Distinct from observability (traces/spans) and analytics (usage trends).

children.name: Research & Experimentation
Ablation Harnesses
children.short_description: Tooling for systematically removing or swapping agent components to attribute behaviour.
children.long_description: Frameworks that make it easy to disable a tool, swap a model, or strip a memory layer and re-run the same scenario — the classic ablation study applied to agent stacks.

children.name: Agent Experimentation Frameworks
children.short_description: Harnesses for running treatment/control experiments on agent configurations.
children.long_description: Frameworks that treat agents as experimental subjects — varying prompt, model, tool set, or memory and running replicated trials to measure the effect on outcomes. The unit of analysis is the agent run.

children.name: Behavioural Probes
children.short_description: Targeted micro-tests that poke at specific agent behaviours in constrained scenarios.
children.long_description: Narrow, hypothesis-driven tests designed to elicit a specific behaviour (sycophancy, tool misuse, goal drift) in a controlled setting. Distinct from broad benchmarks, which aggregate over many tasks.

children.name: Reproducibility Tooling
children.short_description: Tools for capturing and replaying the full state of an agent run so results can be reproduced.
children.long_description: Seed management, deterministic replay, run manifests, and dataset versioning aimed at making agent research reproducible across machines and time.

children.short_description: Frameworks for running controlled studies of agent behaviour — treatments, replications, ablations.
children.long_description: The scientific side of agent evaluation: tools that let researchers design controlled experiments across agent configurations, track independent and dependent variables, replicate runs, and reason about statistical significance. Distinct from Evals and Experiment Tracking (which is production-oriented telemetry) and from Benchmarks (which are fixed leaderboards).

segments.short_description: Telemetry, evaluation, and operational monitoring for LLM and agent systems across their lifecycle.
segments.long_description: Covers benchmarks, eval and experiment-tracking frameworks, runtime observability, and cost/ops monitoring for agents and LLM applications. In scope: tracing, judge frameworks, analytics, SRE-style tooling. Out of scope: safety policy enforcement (see Safety & Governance) and model training infra.

segments.name: Safety & Governance

children.name: Agent in the Loop
Critic & Reviewer Agents
children.short_description: Agents that review another agent's plan, reasoning, or output and return critiques used to revise it.
children.long_description: Reviewer/critic patterns where a second agent audits a first agent's work before it is accepted — plan critique, output review, reflection loops executed by a separate agent rather than self-reflection within the same context.

children.name: Policy Enforcement Agents
children.short_description: Agents that evaluate proposed actions against a policy document and veto or allow them.
children.long_description: Agents configured with an organisational or regulatory policy that other agents must consult before acting. The policy is reasoned about by the enforcement agent rather than compiled into static rules.

children.name: Supervisor Agents
children.short_description: Agents whose role is to approve, reject, or moderate the actions of other agents at runtime.
children.long_description: A supervisory agent sits above a worker agent and gates its tool calls or outputs against a policy. Distinct from Guardrails (which are typically rule- or classifier-based) because the supervisor is itself an LLM agent reasoning about intent and context.

children.short_description: Patterns where a supervisory agent reviews, approves, or moderates another agent's actions before execution.
children.long_description: Architectures that insert an LLM reviewer — a safety critic or policy agent — into another agent's loop to approve tool calls or outputs. Distinct from human-in-the-loop (which routes to people) and static guardrails (which apply rules without reasoning).

children.name: Guardrails
Policy Compilation
children.short_description: Toolchains that compile natural-language policies or constitutions into runtime guardrails.
children.long_description: Tooling that takes a written policy (company rules, a constitution, regulatory text) and turns it into executable guardrails — rule extraction, test generation, and classifier training driven by the policy document.

children.name: Policy Engines & Guardrail Routers
children.short_description: Libraries and routers that enforce content, safety, and behavioral policies on LLM inputs and outputs.
children.long_description: Policy engines intercept prompts and responses to block unsafe content, enforce topic boundaries, and route based on rules. Typically embedded as a library or proxy in front of model calls (e.g. NeMo Guardrails, Llama Guard, Guardrails AI). Sits between the agent runtime and the model.

children.short_description: Runtime filters and policy checks that constrain agent inputs, outputs, or actions against defined rules.
children.long_description: Includes input/output validators, refusal classifiers, command confirmation layers like Shellfirm, and policy routers. Typical interface: middleware wrapping model calls or tool invocations. Distinct from identity/auth and PII redaction, which solve narrower problems.

children.name: Hallucination & Factuality
Citation Verification
children.short_description: Checks that cited sources actually support the claim they're attached to.
children.long_description: Runtime and eval-time tools that fetch cited sources and verify the claim is supported by the text, distinguishing hallucinated citations from real ones.

children.name: Claim Extraction
children.short_description: Tools that decompose LLM outputs into atomic factual claims.
children.long_description: Libraries that parse an agent output into a list of discrete, checkable claims — the prerequisite for downstream verification.

children.name: Consistency Checks
children.short_description: Self-consistency and cross-retrieval consistency checks as hallucination signals.
children.long_description: Sampling multiple generations, or checking an answer against multiple retrievals, and flagging divergence as a hallucination signal.

children.short_description: Tools that detect, measure, or prevent hallucinated claims in agent outputs.
children.long_description: Runtime detectors and evaluation libraries focused specifically on factual grounding of LLM outputs — claim extraction, citation verification, consistency checks across retrievals, and hallucination scores. Distinct from general grounding infra under Context/Data.

children.name: Human in the Loop
Approval Gates
children.short_description: Synchronous checkpoints that block an agent action until a human explicitly approves it.
children.long_description: Pre-action confirmation flows where the agent pauses and the human must allow, deny, or edit a proposed tool call before it runs. Distinct from Review Inboxes (async) and from Interrupt & Takeover (mid-action).

children.name: Clarification Loops
children.short_description: Protocols for agents to ask humans targeted questions when they are uncertain.
children.long_description: Mechanisms letting an agent explicitly request missing information from a human rather than guessing — structured clarification prompts, disambiguation UIs, uncertainty-triggered question generation.

children.name: Correction & Annotation
children.short_description: Tools letting humans correct agent outputs and feed those corrections back for training or retrieval.
children.long_description: Post-hoc correction UIs that capture human edits to agent outputs and route them to eval datasets, fine-tuning pipelines, or memory stores. Distinct from Approval Gates because the action has already happened.

children.name: Interrupt & Takeover
children.short_description: Patterns letting a human jump into a running agent session and take manual control.
children.long_description: Mid-execution intervention — pausing an agent, editing its state or plan, or taking over its session. Used for voice agents, computer-use agents, and long-running autonomous runs.

children.name: Review Inboxes
children.short_description: Async queues where humans review completed or pending agent actions in bulk.
children.long_description: Inbox-style UIs where agent decisions accumulate for later human review, approval, or correction — suited to long-running agents whose work cannot block on a human in real time.

children.short_description: Workflows and UIs that route agent decisions to humans for approval, correction, or escalation.
children.long_description: Review queues, approval gates, and annotator interfaces where a human signs off on risky agent actions or labels ambiguous cases. Distinct from agent-in-the-loop (LLM reviewer) and from pure HITL data labeling, which targets training rather than runtime oversight.

children.name: Identity and Auth
Agent Identity Protocols
children.short_description: Protocols establishing verifiable identity, credentials, and authority for autonomous agents acting on behalf of users.
children.long_description: Open specifications for how agents authenticate themselves to services, carry delegated authority, and prove provenance — e.g. agent passports, agent payment protocols. Protocol-level standards, not SDKs; the plumbing for agent-to-service trust across organizational boundaries.

children.name: Secrets & Vaults for Agents
children.short_description: Vaults and brokers that hold credentials agents need, without exposing them in context.
children.long_description: Services that inject credentials into tool calls at runtime without letting the agent see them, or that issue short-lived tokens scoped to a single agent run. Distinct from general secret managers because the threat model assumes the agent itself may be adversarial or compromised.

children.short_description: Identity, authentication, and delegated-authority mechanisms for agents acting on behalf of users or services.
children.long_description: Covers agent identity protocols, scoped credentials, OAuth flows for agents, and non-human identity management. Typical interface: identity providers, token brokers, or standards. Distinct from guardrails (content policy) and from generic IAM by focusing on agent-specific delegation.

children.name: PII and Redaction
children.short_description: Detecting and redacting personal, sensitive, or regulated data from prompts, traces, and agent outputs.
children.long_description: Tools and libraries that scan text for PII/PHI/PCI and mask or tokenize before it reaches models or logs. Typical interface: middleware or SDK filters. Distinct from general guardrails by targeting data privacy and compliance specifically.

children.name: Provenance & Watermarking
children.short_description: Signals and specs for proving whether content was produced by an agent.
children.long_description: C2PA manifests, synthetic-output watermarking, and cryptographic content credentials. Concerned with the authenticity trail of agent-generated artefacts rather than the agent's runtime behaviour.

children.name: Research & Ethics Evaluations
Alignment Evaluations
children.short_description: Evaluations probing whether an agent's behaviour matches its principal's intent under stress.
children.long_description: Scenario suites and techniques for testing alignment properties — goal-following, corrigibility, deception, power-seeking, reward hacking. Typically adversarial or out-of-distribution probes rather than capability benchmarks.

children.name: Bias & Fairness Evaluations
children.short_description: Test suites and frameworks for measuring bias and fairness in agent decisions and outputs.
children.long_description: Datasets, metrics, and harnesses focused on demographic bias, representational harms, and disparate impact in agent outputs and tool-use decisions. Distinct from general eval suites because the target property is fairness rather than capability.

children.name: Ethics Frameworks
children.short_description: Codified ethical principles, audit checklists, and standards used to evaluate agent deployments.
children.long_description: Structured frameworks — principles, checklists, impact assessments, and audit standards — that organisations apply to agents before or after deployment. Includes ecosystem standards bodies' ethics outputs and independent audit methodologies.

children.name: Interpretability Tools
children.short_description: Tools for inspecting and explaining the internal reasoning of agents and the models behind them.
children.long_description: Mechanistic interpretability libraries, activation probing, chain-of-thought faithfulness checks, and agent-level introspection tools that expose *why* an agent chose an action rather than just *what* it did.

children.name: Model Organism Studies
children.short_description: Deliberately constructed agents used as subjects for safety and alignment research.
children.long_description: Research artefacts where a model or agent is intentionally trained or configured to exhibit a property of interest (sleeper behaviour, deception, sandbagging) so the property can be studied and detection methods validated.

children.name: Safety Benchmarks
children.short_description: Benchmarks whose target property is safe behaviour rather than task success.
children.long_description: Standardised leaderboards that score agents on refusal quality, jailbreak resistance, harmful-tool-use avoidance, and similar safety properties. Kept separate from the general Benchmarks slot because the measurement target is orthogonal to capability.

children.short_description: Tools and frameworks for evaluating alignment, interpretability, bias, and ethical properties of agents.
children.long_description: The research-oriented evaluation stack for agent safety and ethics — distinct from runtime Guardrails (which block bad actions in production) and from general Benchmarks (which measure capability). Covers alignment probes, interpretability of agent reasoning, bias and fairness audits, and frameworks that codify ethical principles into testable criteria.

children.name: Security
Automation
children.short_description: Security automation and SOAR-style platforms adapted for agent workflows and incident response.
children.long_description: Playbook-driven automation for triaging alerts, executing responses, and orchestrating security workflows where agents are either operators or subjects. Distinct from Control Planes (policy enforcement) and Sandboxing (isolation) — this slot is about scripted, repeatable security actions. Example: Tracecat.

children.name: Control Planes
children.short_description: Centralized policy and governance control planes that enforce rules across fleets of agents and tools.
children.long_description: Administrative layers that set, distribute, and enforce security policy across many agents — access control, audit, kill-switches. Sits above individual guardrail libraries, providing org-level governance. Example: Cordum.

children.name: Red Team
Prompt Injection Scanners
children.short_description: Scanners and test harnesses specifically targeting prompt injection and LLM jailbreak vulnerabilities.
children.long_description: Tools that automate discovery of prompt-injection weaknesses in LLM applications via curated attack libraries and fuzzing (Garak, PyRIT, Promptfoo Redteam). A narrow slot under Red Team focused on the input-manipulation attack class, not broader adversarial ML.

children.short_description: Offensive security tooling for probing agents and LLMs for vulnerabilities, jailbreaks, and unsafe behaviors.
children.long_description: Red-team frameworks that attack agent systems to surface weaknesses before adversaries do — fuzzing prompts, chaining exploits, simulating malicious tool use. Distinct from evals (which measure capability) in that the goal is adversarial discovery.

children.name: Sandboxing
children.short_description: Isolated execution environments where agents can run code, commands, or tools without risking the host.
children.long_description: Lightweight VMs, microVMs, or container-based sandboxes purpose-built for agent code execution and tool invocation. Distinct from general dev sandboxes — optimized for short-lived, untrusted LLM-generated workloads. Example: Microsandbox.

children.short_description: Security tooling for agent systems — sandboxing, red-teaming, control planes, and automated defense.
children.long_description: Covers the runtime and operational security layer for agents: isolating execution, detecting malicious inputs, enforcing policy at the control plane, and automating incident response. Distinct from Guardrails (content/policy filtering) and Identity (who the agent is) — this slot is about protecting the system from and around the agent.

segments.short_description: Controls, policies, and oversight mechanisms that keep agents safe, compliant, and accountable in production.
segments.long_description: Covers security, identity/auth, PII redaction, guardrails, and human- or agent-in-the-loop oversight for LLM-driven systems. Typical interface: middleware, policy engines, or review UIs. Distinct from observability: focus is enforcement and intervention, not measurement.

segments.name: Tools & Integrations

children.name: Actions
AI Browsers
Headless
children.short_description: Headless browsers designed for AI agents — no UI, optimized for programmatic navigation and scraping.
children.long_description: Browser engines built or tuned for agent workloads, exposing DOM/automation APIs without a rendered UI. In scope: Lightpanda, Steel Browser and similar headless runtimes. Out of scope: cloud browser infrastructure services and human-facing AI browsers.

children.short_description: Full browser products with native AI/agent capabilities built into the user-facing application.
children.long_description: End-user browsers (Chromium forks or new builds) where agentic features are first-class — not scriptable automation stacks but actual browsers a human or agent drives. Distinct from Browser Automation (programmatic control) and Browser Tooling (libraries). Example: BrowserOS.

children.name: Authenticated Tool Proxies
