children.short_description: Hosted proxies that handle OAuth, credentials, and tool invocation on behalf of agents across many SaaS APIs.
children.long_description: Services that abstract auth and API surfaces so agents can call Gmail, Slack, Notion, etc. without managing tokens or per-API SDKs. Distinct from Integrations (generic connector libs) — the differentiator is managed auth and a unified tool surface. Examples: Arcade, Pica, Nango.

children.name: Browser Automation
Cloud Browser Infrastructure
children.short_description: Hosted browser infrastructure providing managed sessions, proxies, and captchas for agent automation at scale.
children.long_description: Cloud services running fleets of real browsers that agents drive via Playwright/CDP, handling scaling, auth, and anti-bot concerns. In scope: Browserbase, Hyperbrowser, Anchor Browser. Out of scope: local headless browser engines and human-facing AI browsers.

children.short_description: Programmatic browser control stacks aimed at agent-driven automation and scraping workflows.
children.long_description: Automation frameworks and runtimes that drive real browsers under agent control — built on or beside Playwright/CDP. Distinct from AI Browsers (user-facing products) and Browser Tooling (helper libs layered on top). Includes cloud browser infrastructure as a child.

children.name: Browser Tooling
children.short_description: Libraries and helpers layered on browser automation stacks to make them agent-friendly (DOM extraction, action planning).
children.long_description: Thin layers above Playwright/CDP that give agents structured affordances — accessibility snapshots, element selection, observation primitives. Distinct from full Browser Automation runtimes; these are the glue libraries agents use. Examples: Stagehand, Agent Browser.

children.name: Computer Use
CLI
children.short_description: Computer-use agents that drive the machine via CLI/shell rather than GUI interactions.
children.long_description: Runtimes that let agents operate a computer primarily through terminal commands and shell sessions. In scope: CLI-first computer-use harnesses. Out of scope: vision/GUI computer use and OS-specific GUI controllers.

children.name: Platform Specific
macOS
children.short_description: macOS-native computer-use runtimes controlling the Mac via AppleScript, accessibility, or native APIs.
children.long_description: Agent harnesses that drive macOS specifically, using platform automation to control apps and windows. In scope: macOS-use, cmux and similar. Out of scope: cross-platform vision-based computer use and CLI-only harnesses.

children.short_description: Computer-use tooling scoped to a specific operating system, leveraging native APIs for control.
children.long_description: OS-bound computer-use implementations that rely on platform accessibility, scripting, or automation APIs. In scope: macOS-, Windows-, or Linux-specific controllers. Out of scope: cross-platform vision-UI or CLI-based computer use.

children.name: Vision UI
children.short_description: Vision-based computer-use agents that interact with GUIs through screenshots and pixel-level grounding.
children.long_description: Systems using multimodal models to see the screen and emit clicks/keystrokes without DOM or accessibility access. In scope: vision-grounded GUI agents like UI-Venus. Out of scope: CLI-based and platform-native accessibility-driven computer use.

children.short_description: Frameworks letting agents control a full desktop OS via keyboard, mouse, and screen — beyond just the browser.
children.long_description: OS-level agent control: platform-specific drivers, CLI-first approaches, and vision-based UI models. Distinct from Browser Automation in that the surface is the entire desktop/VM, not a web page. Example: cua.

children.name: Data Extraction and Structuring
children.short_description: Services that turn web pages and unstructured sources into clean, structured data for agents.
children.long_description: Crawl-and-extract APIs that convert HTML, PDFs, and messy pages into markdown or JSON for LLM consumption. Typically a hosted API (Firecrawl) rather than a library. Distinct from Browser Automation — the goal is data output, not interaction.

children.name: Financial Tools
Agent Payment Rails
children.short_description: Payment rails and protocols that let agents pay for services, APIs, or other agents programmatically.
children.long_description: Machine-payable infrastructure — settlement protocols, metering, and HTTP-native payment specs for agents. In scope: x402, Skyfire, Nevermined. Out of scope: agent wallets that hold funds and traditional payment APIs not agent-oriented.

children.name: Agent Wallets
children.short_description: Wallet infrastructure giving agents custody, signing, and balance management for on-chain or fiat funds.
children.long_description: Server-side and embedded wallets provisioned per agent to hold keys, sign transactions, and manage balances. In scope: Coinbase CDP AgentKit, Crossmint, Privy server wallets. Out of scope: payment rails/settlement protocols and general consumer wallets.

children.short_description: Toolkits giving agents financial capabilities — wallets, payment rails, and transaction execution.
children.long_description: Libraries and SDKs that let agents hold funds, sign transactions, and move money (crypto or fiat). Split into agent payment rails and agent wallets. Examples: GOAT, Coinbase AgentKit. Distinct from Identity protocols like AP2, which define the handshake rather than the execution.

children.name: Integrations
children.short_description: Generic integration/connector libraries exposing many third-party APIs as agent-callable tools.
children.long_description: SDKs that bundle hundreds of SaaS API connectors into one tool surface for agents (Composio). Distinct from Authenticated Tool Proxies in that the focus is the connector catalog and SDK shape, not managed auth-as-a-service.

children.name: Marketplaces
children.short_description: Marketplaces where agents and their actions are listed, discovered, and transacted by other agents or humans.
children.long_description: Directories and storefronts for publishing or hiring agents and packaged actions. In scope: agent listing sites, discovery portals, commerce layers for agent services. Out of scope: MCP registries (see MCP > Registries & Marketplaces) and tool integration catalogs.

children.name: Screen Understanding
children.short_description: Models and tools that parse screenshots into structured UI elements for computer-use agents.
children.long_description: OCR, GUI element detectors, and vision models that turn a screenshot into a list of clickable regions with labels. The perception layer that sits under Computer Use frameworks — distinct from them because the output is a structured scene graph, not an action.

children.name: Software Extensions
children.short_description: Extensions that embed agentic actions into existing host software such as IDEs, notebooks, or editors.
children.long_description: Plugins and add-ons that expose agent capabilities inside a host application rather than standing alone. In scope: notebook assistants, IDE extensions, editor plugins delivering tool-call actions. Out of scope: standalone agents and browser/computer-use runtimes.

children.name: Upserting Tools
children.short_description: Tools that let agents insert or update records in external systems (CRMs, databases, SaaS) as a write-side action.
children.long_description: The write counterpart to extraction: action tools specialized in upserting structured data back into systems of record. In scope: CRM/DB upsert connectors and writeback utilities exposed to agents. Out of scope: read-only data extraction and general integration catalogs.

children.name: Voice
children.short_description: Voice action tools letting agents place calls, speak, and handle telephony-grade audio interactions.
children.long_description: Libraries and services that give agents voice as an action surface — outbound/inbound calls, IVR, real-time audio. In scope: voice agent SDKs and telephony runtimes used as tools. Out of scope: voice-first agent frameworks (Frameworks > Voice) and raw TTS/STT models.

children.name: Web Search APIs for Agents
children.short_description: Web search APIs purpose-built as agent tool calls, returning LLM-friendly results rather than SERP HTML.
children.long_description: Search-as-a-service endpoints designed for agent consumption: clean snippets, ranked passages, and retrieval-oriented responses. In scope: Tavily, Exa, Linkup, Serper and similar. Out of scope: general scraping, browser automation, and vector retrieval over private corpora.

children.short_description: Concrete action surfaces agents invoke — browsers, computer use, APIs, wallets, voice, and third-party integrations.
children.long_description: The "hands" of the agent: the actual effectors that let it browse, click, pay, extract, or call services. Distinct from MCP (the protocol) and Standards (the specs) — this slot is where real-world capabilities live, grouped by modality and target system.

children.name: MCP
Aggregation
children.short_description: Services that aggregate many MCP servers behind a single unified interface for an agent client.
children.long_description: Aggregators fan out a client's MCP connection across multiple backend servers, multiplexing tools into one session. In scope: multi-server MCP hosts and bundlers. Out of scope: protocol gateways that translate transports, and registries that only list servers.

children.name: Gateways
children.short_description: MCP gateways that proxy, translate, or policy-enforce between MCP clients and backend servers.
children.long_description: Middleware sitting on the wire between MCP client and server — handling transport translation, auth, routing, observability, and policy. In scope: MCP proxies and context-forge style gateways. Out of scope: pure aggregators and server registries.

children.name: Registries & Marketplaces
children.short_description: Registries and marketplaces for discovering, publishing, and installing MCP servers.
children.long_description: Catalogs of MCP servers with metadata, install flows, and sometimes hosted execution. In scope: Smithery, PulseMCP, the official MCP Registry. Out of scope: gateways and aggregators that run servers, and non-MCP agent/action marketplaces.

children.name: Third Party
children.short_description: Third-party MCP servers wrapping external products or APIs that the vendor itself does not ship.
children.long_description: Community-built MCP servers exposing SaaS products, websites, or APIs as tools when no first-party server exists. In scope: independently maintained server implementations for specific services. Out of scope: official/vendor MCP servers and registries listing them.

children.short_description: Model Context Protocol ecosystem — servers, gateways, aggregators, registries, and third-party MCP implementations.
children.long_description: Everything in and around MCP, the emerging standard for exposing tools and context to LLMs. Covers server implementations, gateways that multiplex or proxy MCP traffic, aggregation layers, and marketplaces. Distinct from generic tool libraries — these are specifically MCP-shaped.

children.name: Standards
Emerging (Ecosystem-Tied)
children.short_description: Emerging agent standards implemented inside a specific ecosystem or product rather than a neutral body.
children.long_description: Specs gaining traction through a particular platform or browser vendor before any cross-industry ratification. In scope: WebMCP and similar ecosystem-anchored proposals. Out of scope: fully ratified cross-vendor protocols and early paper-only proposals.

children.name: Proposed
children.short_description: Proposed agent-ecosystem standards still in draft — not yet widely implemented or ratified.
children.long_description: Early-stage specifications circulated for feedback: protocols, certifications, or interoperability drafts without committed adopters yet. In scope: draft specs like GitAgent or AIUC-1. Out of scope: ratified protocols and specs already shipping inside a specific ecosystem.

children.name: Ratified
children.short_description: Ratified, broadly adopted agent interoperability protocols backed by multi-vendor implementations.
children.long_description: Published specs with stable versions and shipping implementations across the ecosystem. In scope: MCP, A2A, AG-UI and similar. Out of scope: draft proposals and ecosystem-tied emerging specs.

children.short_description: Open standards and specifications for agent interoperability — ratified, proposed, and ecosystem-tied protocols.
children.long_description: Specification-level artifacts defining how agents, tools, and services interoperate. Segmented by maturity: ratified standards, proposals under discussion, and emerging standards tightly coupled to a single vendor's ecosystem. Distinct from MCP, which has its own dedicated branch.

segments.short_description: Tools, actions, and integration layers that connect agents to the outside world — MCP, standards, and action surfaces.
segments.long_description: The top-level domain for everything that extends an agent's reach: tool protocols (MCP), standards bodies, and the concrete action surfaces agents invoke (browsers, APIs, wallets, voice). Sits between frameworks/runtimes and the real-world systems agents manipulate.
